Thanks. I guess you are using pgi just for travis and pygobject for anything else?

Have a look at how to use pygobject on travis: https://pygobject.readthedocs.org/en/latest/testing.html#example-travis-ci-configuration

For the error itself, I can't reproduce here, but will keep this open in case I see something related
(or you can provide a reproducer?)
LGTM :+1:

Hmm. What does the following command show? It could be that one of the dependencies is not working

$ dRep bonus test --check_dependencies

To be more specific, installing `drep` from bioconda creates the following error:

```
***************************************************
    ..:: dRep dereplicate Step 1. Filter ::..
***************************************************

Will filter the genome list
Calculating genome info of genomes
100.00% of genomes passed length filtering
Running prodigal
Running checkM
Program checkm is not working!! Im going to crash now
```

I'm guessing this is due to the initial database setup for `checkm`. If I try and run checkm to initialize the setup, then I get the following:

```
$ checkm
  File "/ebio/abt3_projects/databases/miniconda3/envs/metaphlan2_db/bin/checkm", line 58
    print ''
           ^
SyntaxError: Missing parentheses in call to 'print'. Did you mean print('')?
```

With python2:

```
$ python2 $(which checkm)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/ebio/abt3_projects/databases/miniconda3/envs/metaphlan2_db/bin/checkm", line 36, in <module>
    from checkm import main
ImportError: No module named checkm
```

So `checkm` is currently not working with `drep` for the bioconda install.

Hello,

A couple of questions: 

1) Could you please provide the command you ran that generated the error? 

2) Could you please attach the log file? It's located in `log/logger.log`

Thanks,
-Matt

In all case it seems it starts directly with centrifuge but not prodigal.

Agreed, I just updated the message to avoid confusion.

Potentially not a problem for only me. 

Here's one scenario where this would break things:

Rita has her own python install (virtualenv or full install), she installs URH and the temp files for the native driver are written to /tmp

Tina has her own python install (virtualenv or full install), she installs URH and the temp files for the native driver are written to /tmp but the process fails because Rita's files are in /tmp

Maybe it's okay to leave them but they should all then be written into a subdir of /tmp/   e.g. /tmp/rita-urh

There are other reasons to remove them altogether after building:

- Some systems do not clear /tmp on reboot
- Some systems rarely reboot and /tmp begins to be cluttered over time
- Some systems if put /tmp into ramfs or similar volatile filesystem that has space limits/constraints

https://github.com/berdario/pew/blob/master/pew/shell_config/init.bash

if you've installed one of the latest versions of pew on a new machine, you should've seen that it proposes to modify your .bashrc (or .zshrc...) for you.

Again, this is just for convenience and to be less confusing for new users, it's indeed not part of pew core.

I'm willing to change the default to something else, but it has to be (just like the one that it's shipping now) simple, so no `awesome-fontconfig`.

Expect it is there to avoid the problem @imrn mentions -- re-using the same port eg when you are working on code that runs a monitor.    One useful side-effect is that if you fire a second one up using the same port it seems to wait until any previous process that is using that port exits.

Also @hgrecco and @endolith, there needs to be something said about multiplication/division between logarithmic units. `dBm * dBm` does not really make sense, but internally, it would really be `dB-(mW**2)`. 

Another thing to take into consideration, what if the user wanted dB-meters? If the paradigm is `dB-meter = 1 * m; logbase: 10`, would the user have to specify the `dB` unit for every quantity? I wouldn't use `pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL` because that is the default in Python3 (with some caveats). When Python2 goes by the wayside in 2020,  specifying the protocol will no longer be necessary (at least for `__slots__`). If `six` automatically uses the highest protocol, then less code will need to be migrated. Likewise, `__getstate__` and `__setstate__` methods for Python2 may not be necessary in Python3 because of the default protocol change.

By the way, I now have callbacks working and I hope to publish the whole package soon. A callback can be added to report changes on analog input, digital input, i2c input, encoders, and for digital and analog latches. Adding a callback to a latch, will essentially give you a one-shot. For example, if you set an analog latch to fire when the value for the pin is greater than 500, when that value is achieved, the callback is invoked and the latch is cleared. It can easily be rearmed anywhere in the application program, including the callback function.

Call backs will return the input type, pin number and value. For i2c, instead of pin number the device address is returned. For the latching callbacks, a time stamp of when the event occurred is provided as well.

You can have a single callback function to receive all callbacks, or you can set the granularity to be one callback function per pin.

Want to get rid of the callback, just call set_pin_mode without the callback function specified and the call back is disabled.

Hi Pedro,
the "fake" server is  embedded in the Mock strategy so it's completely included in the client side. In your case you can define the connection in your subprocess and the server will "live" in the same subprocess. You don't need to care about the TCP/IP session, all you have to do is to populate the fake server DIT as explained in the ldap3 documentation. This doesn't works  if you must access the same server from two or more subprocesses. If this is your case you must use a different approach, where each subprocess uses its own fake server that is synchronized with the other (as a multi-replica scenario).

Let me know what is your intended use.

Yep, this has been dropped. For this kind of analysis I would use [`pybedtools`](https://daler.github.io/pybedtools/index.html) instead. 

See the [`gffutils.pybedtools_integration.tsses`](http://daler.github.io/gffutils/autodocs/gffutils.pybedtools_integration.tsses.html#gffutils.pybedtools_integration.tsses) for getting TSSs from a FeatureDB and converting to a BedTool; from there you'll probably want to use `BedTool.closest(D="a", io=True)` and filter on the distance reported in the last column.

Here's something to get you started:

```python
import gffutils
import pybedtools
from gffutils.pybedtools_integration import tsses

# small example file
fn = gffutils.example_filename('gencode-v19.gtf')
db = gffutils.create_db(
    fn,
    ":memory:",
    keep_order=True,
    disable_infer_genes=True,
    disable_infer_transcripts=True)

# see docs for details; you'll probably want to customize
x = tsses(db)

# negative distances mean upstream; ignore overlapping. 
y = x.closest(x, D="a", io=True)

# depending on what you want to do with it, either convert to dataframe
# for downstream stuff...
df = y.to_dataframe()

# ...or keep only tsses whose closest TSS is farther than
# some distance upstream
def far_enough_upstream(f, dist):
    d = int(f[-1])
    return (d < 0) and (d < -dist)

z = y.filter(far_enough_upstream, 100).cut(range(9)).saveas()

```

I've been very patient with you guys, trying to explain things to you.  But you have no idea what you are talking about so it's tough.  In particular, you keep saying that the way dlib behaves is some kind of oddity, but in fact its use of software contracts is standard practice.  To help you understand, here are some examples I suggest you study.

In Visual Studio, the runtime libraries (including things like the STL) have both a debug and release version.  If you compile in release mode there are no checks on things like std::vector::operator[].  However, if you compile in debug mode Visual Studio uses a different version of the STL that includes many runtime checks for things like bounds checking on std::vector::operator[].  In their case, they check them with asserts and programs that violate those checks are terminated, but only in debug mode.  You can also read about how design by contract is done in a huge number of other contexts and languages on wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Design_by_contract.  One notable excerpt is the following:

>Contract conditions should never be violated during execution of a bug-free program. Contracts are therefore typically only checked in debug mode during software development. Later at release, the contract checks are disabled to maximize performance.

I would also suggest reading the Effective C++ books by Scott Meyers.  While not specifically about contract programming, they nevertheless contain a lot of good information about it. To get you started, here is a relevant passage about contract programming from Effective Modern C++ discussing contracts and how you might check them with exceptions:

>Now suppose that f(std::string) has a precondition: the length of its std::string parameter doesn't exceed 32 characters.  If f were to be called with a std::string whose length is greater than 32, behavior would be undefined, because a precondition violation by definition results in undefined behavior. f is under no obligation to check this precondition, because functions may assume that their preconditions are satisfied. (Callers are responsible for ensuring that such assumptions are valid.)  
>
>But suppose that f's implementer chooses to check for precondition violations. Checking isn't required, but it's also not forbidden, and checking preconditions could be useful, e.g., during system testing.  Debugging an exception that's been thrown is generally easier than trying to track down the cause of undefined behavior. 

To summarize, the idea of preconditions/contracts is a fundamental concept in software development.  It is widely understood that if you violate a function's preconditions that you invoke undefined behavior.  While I appreciate your enthusiasm, it is not sensible to complain on github about this.  The right thing to do is to write your software so that you don't violate function preconditions, thereby avoiding undefined behavior.  You should also read some software engineering books to better understand these general design principles that underlie most modern software libraries.  Reading the material I mentioned above would be a good place to start.

I realize this whole thing seems excessive but I get emailed daily by people using dlib who have basically no idea what they are doing and this kind of stuff makes a difference in usability for such people.  In fact, dlib didn't start out with the "kill your program on the second ignored fatal error" behavior.  I added it after I kept seeing people write try{} catch() { // do nothing} blocks that caused bugs that *were being detected* by dlib to go unnoticed and unfixed.  Since adding this to dlib this kind of user error has ceased.  

I also don't want people to rely on fatal_error being thrown for the correct behavior of their program (e.g. as some kind of user input validation).  It would be easy to write a program and to only test it in debug mode, which always generates fatal_errors.  Then when debug is turned off for the final product suddenly things don't work because the fatal_errors go away.  Having this kind of belligerent behavior in dlib also prevents that.

@lorenzob , looks like you are detecting with about to 96x64 pixels detection window size (one FHOG cell is about 8x8 pixels and you have 12x8 detector). Your original image has size of  591x434 pixels. So the detection process will work after downscaling the image to ~99x72 pixels (~6x downscale) with the accuracy ±8 pixels. After upscaling the detection result, this ±8 pixels will become ~±48 pixels - this is the result you really have. 

Real accuracy is slightly better because of multiscale image pyramid, but this is the main reason of why your detector does not produce pixel-wise accuracy - your detection window is small and you are detecting large object 

One other reason is the absence of any margins around the detection object on your training images, so it is hard for detector to fit well. If you really don't have any margins and you know that there is right object - why do you need to detect? Just use full image 

So I recommend you to:
1. make rectangles landmarks on training images with imglab tool. this is already done by you
2. extend your training dataset  by distorting source images with small rotation, translation (add margins by replicating the border), scaling and may be some color changes. there is no ready-to-use code for this procedure. it's up on you - try to imitate real images
3. train object_detector 
4. re-detect training images with trained detector and replace rectanges drawn by hand with detected rectangles. this can improve accuracy if your dataset has less than 10k images
5. train shape_predictor with at least 1000 images. you can extend your dataset with distorting training images
6. repeat steps 3-5 with different training parameters until you will have good results

1. Establish standard to be compared against for a given hardware platform, supervisor, and function
2. Gather facts from device (either through SNMP or textfsm/regex parsing), which are, hardware platform, supervisor information, and function of device (assumed from metadata, likely the naming convention of the hostname)
3. Compare facts to the standard and decide if device requires an upgrade or not.
4. If device requires an upgrade, copy the relevant file(s) from a fileserver, change boot statements, removing all but the current running and the new one, check to see if space on primary, and other storage locations, and copy the file if proper space, otherwise clean up space. 
5. Verify md5 hash from step 1 standards, of copied file(s)
6. Mark device as ready for reload and service impacting portion
7. When change window is ready, capture a list of show commands into textfsm outputs (pre-upgrade state)
8. Reload machine (if multiple supervisor, reload all of them)
9. Send post-reload commands, note any differences between pre/post, possibly flagging certain commands as more critical than others
10. Verify running new image and mark device as now in standard.

From https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms644967(v=vs.85).aspx :

> The hook procedure should process a message in less time than the data entry specified in the `LowLevelHooksTimeout` value in the following registry key:
> `HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Control Panel\Desktop`
> The value is in milliseconds. If the hook procedure times out, the system passes the message to the next hook. However, on Windows 7 and later, the hook is silently removed without being called. There is no way for the application to know whether the hook is removed.

(I don't have this value in my registry, so I don't know what kind of magnitude it is)

This may explain why it stops working after a while. When you couple the slowness of Python, a large number of hotkeys to be checked for matches, and high usage from user applications, it's easy to see the timeout being exceeded.

The alternative is to process hotkey matches in yet another thread, but that removes the ability to block keys (`add_hotkey(..., blocking=True)`). This parameter has given me a lot of trouble before, so it might be for the best.

Do you mean the extents of the "song" (whatever that might mean)?

> Detection of the respective first or last boundary is a truly non-trivial task (e.g., fade-in/out), so to my mind these boundaries should be included in the evaluation. 

Yes, I absolutely agree.  Maybe a compromise here is to run through the data and figure out which tracks have leading silence, and only for those do start/end trimming?  It's methodologically ugly to mix results in this way, but it would work.

If you're worried about consistency, I'd recommend mangling the salami data by padding the audio (and annotations) with begin/end silence so that all tracks are on even footing, and the scores can be reported in a unified way.

> Of course, if the temporal tolerance is high enough (e.g., 3 seconds) most of these transitions will practically coincide with file extents, but the choice of tolerance is another issue. 

> 2) The number of boundaries for many pieces is really low. Stripping two of them voluntarily increases statistical variance considerably. For some pieces there might even not be any other annotated boundaries than just these.

A couple of thoughts here:

1) If start and end are retained, everyone gets two hits for free.  This does mean that variance is reduced, but it also increases bias.  (And, as we note in the mir_eval paper, it reduces the power of comparisons by artificially narrowing the effective range of scores.) Since the contribution to the score depends on the number of total boundaries, which varies across tracks, correcting for this bias post-hoc is exceedingly tricky.  I think it's better to just drop these trivial boundaries from the evaluation up-front.

1.5) Dropping the "trivial" boundaries has to be done within the metric, and not as a pre-processing step of the annotations.  This is because mangling the annotations A) won't be consistent across different estimators, and B) structure annotation metrics (pairwise-f, nce) need the information encoded in those boundaries, and are not subject to the triviality bias.

2) If there are no non-trivial boundaries in the piece, why are we evaluating on it?  I think even if a method does poorly in this use-case, no real-world user would care very much.

Last night was the first time I am aware of the *thumb* parameter. Its addition requires me to cram one more file to the call to `_api_request_with_file()`, whose original version is this:

```python
def _api_request_with_file(self, method, params, file_key, file_value, **kwargs):
    if _isstring(file_value):
        params[file_key] = file_value
        return self._api_request(method, _rectify(params), **kwargs)
    else:
        files = {file_key: file_value}
        return self._api_request(method, _rectify(params), files, **kwargs)
```

Bot API allows `file_value` to be either a string (serving as a file id which refers to an existing file on Telegram servers) or a local file to be uploaded. That's why I have to distinguish between `file_value` being a string or not above. If it is a string, merge it to `params`. If not a string, I assume it's a file and pass it separately. Note that variable `files` is a dict.

Now, I want `_api_request_with_file()` to be able to handle multiple files. I would squeeze `file_key` and `file_value` into one parameter of *dict*:

```python
def _api_request_with_file(self, method, params, files, **kwargs):
    params.update({
        k:v for k,v in files.items() if _isstring(v) })

    files = {
        k:v for k,v in files.items() if v is not None and not _isstring(v) }

    return self._api_request(method, _rectify(params), files, **kwargs)
```

I hate sprinkling trivial comments among code, so I explain here:

- `params.update({ k:v for k,v in files.items() if _isstring(v) })` basically extracts all string values from `files` and merges them with `params`
- `files = { k:v for k,v in files.items() if v is not None and not _isstring(v) }` basically extracts all non-null non-string values from `files` and reassigns them to the variable `files`

With this change to `_api_request_with_file()` and the addition of the `thumb` parameter, sendAnimation should looks like this:

```python
def sendAnimation(self, chat_id, animation,
                  duration=None,
                  width=None,
                  height=None,
                  thumb=None,
                  caption=None,
                  parse_mode=None,
                  disable_notification=None,
                  reply_to_message_id=None,
                  reply_markup=None):
    """
    See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendanimation

    :param video: Same as ``photo`` in :meth:`telepot.Bot.sendPhoto`
    """
    p = _strip(locals(), more=['animation', 'thumb'])
    return self._api_request_with_file('sendAnimation',
                                       _rectify(p),
                                       {'animation': animation, 'thumb': thumb})
```

**If you don't mind being a bit venturesome, let me suggest one more change regarding the function `_strip()`.** Instead of simply removing certain parameters from `locals()`, I want it to package them into a separate dict, so I can pass it directly.

Originally:

```python
def _strip(params, more=[]):
    return {key: value for key,value in params.items() if key not in ['self']+more}
```

I would change it to:

```python
def _strip(params, files=[]):
    return (
        { k:v for k,v in params.items() if k not in ['self']+files },
        { k:v for k,v in params.items() if k in files })
```

Then, sendAnimation becomes:

```python
def sendAnimation(self, chat_id, animation,
                  duration=None,
                  width=None,
                  height=None,
                  thumb=None,
                  caption=None,
                  parse_mode=None,
                  disable_notification=None,
                  reply_to_message_id=None,
                  reply_markup=None):
    """
    See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendanimation

    :param video: Same as ``photo`` in :meth:`telepot.Bot.sendPhoto`
    """
    p,f = _strip(locals(), files=['animation', 'thumb'])
    return self._api_request_with_file('sendAnimation', _rectify(p), _rectify(f))
```

Remember to add `thumb` parameter to all other relevant methods. For example, sendVideo should look like:

```python
def sendVideo(self, chat_id, video,
              duration=None,
              width=None,
              height=None,
              thumb=None,
              caption=None,
              parse_mode=None,
              supports_streaming=None,
              disable_notification=None,
              reply_to_message_id=None,
              reply_markup=None):
    """
    See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendvideo

    :param video: Same as ``photo`` in :meth:`telepot.Bot.sendPhoto`
    """
    p,f = _strip(locals(), files=['video', 'thumb'])
    return self._api_request_with_file('sendVideo', _rectify(p), _rectify(f))
```

**Because of the change to `_strip`'s return value, all calls to it should be re-examined** and changed to:

```python
p,f = _strip(locals())
```

Please also note that *none of the above* has been tested, although I am confident that they should not be far off.

```python
def show_all_colors():
    """show all fore,back colors and their combinations"""

    print("# maybe u will see nothing in IDE's console!!! #")
    print('#'+' '*46+'#')

    fore_clr_names = [clr for clr in list(Fore.__dict__.keys())

    back_clr_names = [clr for clr in list(Back.__dict__.keys())
                          if not clr.startswith('__') and clr != 'RESET']

    #show all combinitions as following.
    from itertools import product

    for i, clr_name in enumerate(fore_clr_names, 1):
        clr_code = getattr(Fore, clr_name)
        #remind users this one is black in case he will get confused.
        if clr_name == 'BLACK':
            print(clr_code + Back.WHITE + clr_name + ' this one is black!'
                  + Fore.RESET + Back.RESET)
            continue

        #use blank lines to seperate fore_colors and back's
        if i == len(fore_clr_names):
            print(clr_code + clr_name + Fore.RESET + Back.RESET + '\n\n')
            continue

        print(clr_code + clr_name + Fore.RESET + Back.RESET + '|||', end='')

    for i, clr_name in enumerate(back_clr_names, 1):
        clr_code = getattr(Back, clr_name)

        #use blank lines to seperate back_colors and combinations
        if i == len(back_clr_names):
            print(clr_code + clr_name + Fore.RESET + Back.RESET + '\n\n')
            continue

        print(clr_code + clr_name + Fore.RESET + Back.RESET + '|||', end='')

    for clr_combs in product(fore_clr_names, back_clr_names):
        fore_clr, back_clr = clr_combs

        msg = '{fore} ON {back}'.format(fore=fore_clr, back=back_clr)
        fore_code, back_code = getattr(Fore, fore_clr), getattr(Back, back_clr)

        print(fore_code + back_code + msg + Fore.RESET + Back.RESET + '|||', end='')

if __name__ == "__main__":
    from colorama import init as colorama_init
    colorama_init()
    show_all_colors()
```

Looked at the gssapi authentication code a bit more closely. I believe the issue here is that the gssapi authentication protocol can involve multiple token exchanges, and the auth process should handle that continuously until the context is marked complete.

But there appears to be a bug in `_try_authenticate_gssapi` where the context can be deemed complete when it isn't. Specifically, this log message:
```
DEBUG:kafka.conn:: Successful GSSAPI handshake for vneeruko@DEMO.INTERNAL.LOCAL
```
appears to not have a corresponding `Security Context complete` log message. This means that the context was not complete but the process stopped and marked authentication as done.

I believe I have a fix for this that I will put up as a separate PR.

RQ does not execute jobs inside the same process.

1. The `worker.work(burst=True)` definition: https://github.com/nvie/rq/blob/master/rq/worker.py#L391
2. The actual call to get work done: https://github.com/nvie/rq/blob/master/rq/worker.py#L431
3. Which leads to: https://github.com/nvie/rq/blob/master/rq/worker.py#L488-L514

Notice the `os.fork()` just after the docstring? Yeah, not the same process.

Either way, rom wasn't designed for entities to be passed into any remote job queues - which is what RQ is. Even if the worker were running in the same process, rom doesn't do serialization/deserialization of entities. And if RQ didn't complain when you dropped your just-created object in the queue*, then RQ is doing the wrong things to pass data around.

If you want to pass a rom entity through RQ (or any other task queue system), and you know that it is for a specific model, you should do what I do: put the primary key in the queue (which defaults to the `id` attribute when you don't explicitly include a `PrimaryKey()` attribute), then use `Model.get(primary_key)` in the job.

* I didn't install RQ, nor did I run the code you provided. Everything that I know about RQ I learned from reading the source code for RQ just now.

Closing this because it is not a bug with rom.

I think the root cause of the above issue is because Dynomite supports EVAL/EVALSHA with at least one key.

From: https://github.com/Netflix/dynomite/blob/dev/notes/redis.md
_EVAL and EVALSHA support is limited to scripts that take at least 1 key. If multiple keys are used, all keys must hash to the same server. You can ensure this by using the same hashtag for all keys. If you use more than 1 key, the proxy does no checking to verify that all keys hash to the same server, and the entire command is forwarded to the server that the first key hashes to_

For example:

`127.0.0.1:8102> eval "return redis.call('set','foo','bar')" 0
Error: Server closed the connection
127.0.0.1:8102> eval "return redis.call('set',KEYS[1],'bar')" 1 foo
OK
127.0.0.1:8102>`

It's not even about the different json packages, it's that json is just a format, and the different libraries are just serializers. There is no particularly good reason to choose json for the columns over something like msgpack, thrift, protocol buffers, or even Python's pickle - depending on your needs.

Teach a man to fish, as they say. Now you (and anyone else) can have arbitrarily encoded columns if necessary, and it's documented ;)

I suppose any way that gets it done is as good as the next. But all these operations are possible "within" `python-pptx`, although they're mostly not part of the API. Because they're not API items, they're subject to change between versions without deprecation notice. However, that is actually rare these days as the package has become pretty stable.

The key part of the code you'd need to use is `pptx.opc`. OPC stands for Open Packaging Convention and deals with all the details of getting each of the 'parts' (zip file member files roughly) into the right place with the right relationships.

That would allow you to locate the viewProps part, probably by content type or maybe relationship type from the presentation part. Then you could use `lxml` (XPath maybe) to modify the XML it contains. I don't believe it is parsed yet, so you'd need to look at XmlPart to see how that's done. The raw XML text is in the `.blob` attribute of the part. You can get it from there then put it back before saving after you've made the edits.

Then the relationships belong to the viewProps part, so you can sort through those and remove whichever ones you don't want.

Let me know if you're keen and I can help you find the bits you need. A deluxe approach would be to "monkey-patch" in a ViewPropsPart(XmlPart) object you write which would take care of loading it for you and give you access to the root element. You can even patch in additional custom element classes for the XML elements if you were inclined. That makes the XPath and certain other things easier by obviating the need for Clark-style namespace specification.

Hello @pedro-nonfree,

Well this behaviour is expected when running with **mode hide** only imho (I can't reproduce when doing full screen or switching to tty).

This is not related to py3status but is instead a feature of i3bar.
- When the bar gets hidden, the i3bar code sends a SIGSTOP to the whole process (py3status included) so everything is freezed.
- When the bar is unhidden, a SIGCONT is sent and the process continues.

This is here : http://code.stapelberg.de/git/i3/commit/?id=a83e7699e75cad515019aa1ad5f7d59ee236a918

The only workaround I could think of regarding pomodoro would be to calculate the remaining seconds based on a timestamp instead of a simple decrementation of an interger. But this couldn't beat the fact that you'd get no notification since the whole process is freezed by i3bar.

Hope this helps mate.

Hi Suresh,

I'm not entirely clear what the question is here. YANG is a schema description language, JSON is a data serialisation format. So when you say "convert YANG to JSON" - what are you expecting to need to ingest? A JSON document whose schema is described in YANG?

If that is the case, then yes, you can use PyangBind to load that JSON document into bindings that are generated by PyangBind, and then you are free to access the structures that it stores. You'll probably find the `xpathhelper` classes useful for navigating that data.

If you need to read an arbitrary YANG file at run-time, then PyangBind isn't designed to do that - you'll probably need [pyang](https://github.com/mbj4668/pyang) if you want Python, or [goyang](https://github.com/openconfig/goyang) if you are OK with Go.

Okay, so this is beginning to take shape, although there is lots more work to do!

When we call a RayTracing class, we pass it its image-plane coordinates using the PlaneCoordinates class (this groups the different coordinate sets, e.g. the image-grid, sub-grid, sparse-grid, blurring-region pixels etc..) We then use these PlaneCoordinates to set up an ImagePlane, with which we attach galaxies. Those ImagePlane galaxies are used in its constructor to compute the deflection angles at the image PlaneCoordinates.

The RayTracing class defines the number of lenses and sources, e.g. the most basic class is TraceLensSource, providing a single len-source system. It has a function, 'trace_to_next_plane', which uses the ImagePlane coordinates and deflection angles to trace the to the next pane, which for a lens-source system forms a SourcePlane. For a lens-source system the SourcePlane is the last plane, so we don't need to set up its deflection angles, only its LightProfile and Pixelization.

There will next be a TraceLensSourceCosmology class, which the above class inherites from. This class pretty much does the exact same as above, but sets up cosomology related information using an input set of cosmological parameters.

If our RayTracing class is a TraceMultiPlane, this means we have multiple lens galaxies (as many as we pass in). In this case, we set up the ImagePlane as before, but when we call 'trace_to_next_plane', there are two differences:

1) If the next galaxies is not the last one, a LensPlane instead of SourcePlane is set up. a LensPlane acts the same as an ImagePlane, in that it computes deflection angles, for the next ray-trace. However, unlike the Image-Plane, it is not on the uniform grid of image-coordinates.

2) The function 'trace_to_next_plane' now incorporates galaxy redshift and cosmological information to perform ray-tracing, as for multi-plane lens systems you must factor in the ratios of angulaar diameter distances between all the galaxies.

Once you know every set of Image, Lens and SourcePlanes, it is trivial to calculate either their image-plane model source images (via the Galaxy.LightProfiles) or their pixel-grid mapping matrices (via the Galaxy.Pixelization). These quantities will thus be generated in the DataModel layer, where things like the PSF will be available for convolution and the ImageData will be available for fitting the model.

**Outstanding Issues**

- The different structure of coordinate arrays (e.g. image pixels as a np.shape = (image_pixels, 2) and sub-pixels are np,shape (image_pixels, sub_grid_size**2, 2) means calling the routines like galaxy.compute_deflection_angles_at_coordinates() have become very messy. We need to make these array_like, in that we can pass them a numpy array of any shape and they'll return a numpy array of the same shape.

- We're juggling a lot of 'None' types in the RayTracing class, as one often cannot be sure what sets of coordinates are needed (e.g. you don't always need a sparse_grid, in which case sparse_coordinates -= None) and galaxies may often have mass_profiles=None, or light_profiles=None when we pass them to functions. Ill try and make sure our unit tests robustly define the desired behaviour, but this is again an area which needs better syntax than currently there.

- We need to figure out a clear way to assign a galaxy to the ImagePlane, even though its redshift corresponds to a higher redshift LensPlane or SourcePlane. I discuss this in https://github.com/Jammy2211/PyAutoLens/issues/11 , and the premise is that because an ImagePlane is on a uniform grid of coordinates we can compute a galaxy's LightProfile faster and more accurately exploiting this uniformity. 

I think I will add a bool to the Galaxy class, e.g. 'fit_light_profile_in_image_plane', which when True means that the galaxy's light profile is passed to the ImagePlane class in a new attribute (e.g. 'higher_redshift_light_profiles'). Its light profile is then NOT passed to its actual LensPlane or SourcePlane. This still feels very messy though!

Okay, we need to think carefully about how the hyper-parameters are set up. There will be different pixeliations with different combinations of hyper-parameters. For example, a pixelization without weighted KMeans, would just have a regularization coefficient, whereas a pixelization with weighting would have the regularization coefficient + two parameters that control clustering.

Do we want to define these parameters with the pixelizations, or have a HyperParameters class?

Remember, there are also hyper-parameters associated with thee scaled noise maps, so defined outside the source pixelization. I think a HyperParameters class is cleaner, with the source pixelizations receiving their weighted points for KMeans clustering, which were already computed in the HyperParameters class.

The problem is that ElectrumX is trying to build a raw block for a legacy daemon and doesn't get the expected JSON reply. It seems that you are running Bitbay and it is defined [here](https://github.com/kyuupichan/electrumx/blob/master/lib/coins.py#L1023) as:
```
class Bitbay(ScryptMixin, Coin):
    NAME = "Bitbay"
    SHORTNAME = "BAY"
    NET = "mainnet"
    P2PKH_VERBYTE = bytes.fromhex("19")
    P2SH_VERBYTES = [bytes.fromhex("55")]
    WIF_BYTE = bytes.fromhex("99")
    GENESIS_HASH = ('0000075685d3be1f253ce777174b1594'
                    '354e79954d2a32a6f77fe9cba00e6467')
    DAEMON = daemon.LegacyRPCDaemon # <------- this could be wrong
    TX_COUNT = 4594999
    TX_COUNT_HEIGHT = 1667070
    TX_PER_BLOCK = 3
    RPC_PORT = 19914
    REORG_LIMIT = 5000
```
Maybe the Bitbay daemon was updated and now supports the compatible `getblock` RPC call. Try calling `getblock <a-block-hash> false` and check if the output is a hex string. If that is the case, then the following line must be removed:
```
DAEMON = daemon.LegacyRPCDaemon
```

If I even understand properly what you are trying to do, you need to override the special ``__getitem__()`` method via the wrapper. So use:

```
import wrapt

class Proxy(wrapt.ObjectProxy):
    def __getitem__(self, key):
        if key == 'a':
            return 'bar'
        return self.__wrapped__[key]

d = dict(a="foo")

proxy = Proxy(d)
```

There's nothing fundamentally different when working with 10k than 2. You'd probably want to collect the futures in an array and then use something like `as_completed` to iterate over them as they finish.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor, as_completed
from requests import Session
from requests_futures.sessions import FuturesSession

session = FuturesSession(executor=ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=10),
                         session=Session())

urls = ['https://httpbin.org/get?i={}'.format(i) for i in range(5)]

futures = []
for url in urls:
    future = session.get(url, timeout=5)
    futures.append(future)

for future in as_completed(futures):
    # you may want to handle exceptions here
    resp = future.result()
    print(resp.request.url, resp.status_code)
```

In https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-dev/2017-November/150883.html, @gvanrossum suggested matching the fields as well as their values. This means that a class and a subclass that doesn't add any fields could be compared, but if a subclass adds fields it will not be comparable. This comes down to:

```
isinstance(other, self.__class__) and len(fields(other)) == len(fields(self)) and <all individual fields match>
```

You can just compare `len(fields(obj))` because a subclass will always start with the same number of fields as the base class, by definition. So if the len's don't match, the subclass must have added some fields.

I think this is a good solution, and provides the functionality that we're looking for. I'll come up with a PR shortly.

You see that PyMongo checks the server every 10 seconds and the server responds, but the PyMongo thread that is trying to insert is blocked.

Follow the tutorial to see how to use MockupDB for useful tasks:

http://mockupdb.readthedocs.io/tutorial.html#reply-to-write-commands

I do not know the process behind creating these installers, but here are my two cents anyways: I think ideally they would just be attached to the usual releases resp. tags. For example when you release e.g. `2017.5.0`, it could have the two source archives and the Window installer. And even though there is no need to make a full release if only the installer needs to be updated, it should not hurt either? For example, one could just tag the same commit that is `2017.5.0` as `2017.5.1` to have a separate release without the fear of regressions (if the installer is created independently of any repository code changes that is). If the change log says that this is just an installer update, packagers can skip it if they want. (If the installer is updated via code changes in the repository, it is probably simpler to just bite the bullet and make another release instead of fumbling around with maintenance branches.)
Indeed, this approach is much better!

Use this code instead.

```

import re
import PyV8
from urlparse import urlparse
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter

DEFAULT_USER_AGENT = ("Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) "
                      "Ubuntu Chromium/34.0.1847.116 Chrome/34.0.1847.116 Safari/537.36")

class CloudflareAdapter(HTTPAdapter):
    def send(self, request, **kwargs):
        domain = request.url.split("/")[2]
        resp = super(CloudflareAdapter, self).send(request, **kwargs)

        # Check if we already solved a challenge
        if request._cookies.get("cf_clearance", domain="." + domain):
            return resp

        # Check if Cloudflare anti-bot is on
        if "a = document.getElementById('jschl-answer');" in resp.content:
            return self.solve_cf_challenge(resp, request.headers, **kwargs)

        # Otherwise, no Cloudflare anti-bot detected
        return resp

    def add_headers(self, request):
        # Spoof Chrome on Linux if no custom User-Agent has been set
        if "requests" in request.headers["User-Agent"]:
            request.headers["User-Agent"] = DEFAULT_USER_AGENT

    def solve_cf_challenge(self, resp, headers, **kwargs):
        headers = headers.copy()
        url = resp.url
        parsed = urlparse(url)
        domain = parsed.netloc
        page = resp.content
        kwargs.pop("params", None) # Don't pass on params

        try:
            # Extract the arithmetic operation
            challenge = re.search(r'name="jschl_vc" value="(\w+)"', page).group(1)
            builder = re.search(r"setTimeout.+?\r?\n([\s\S]+?a\.value =.+?)\r?\n", page).group(1)
            builder = '\n'.join(builder.split('\n')[12:]) #Here's what I changed.
            builder = re.sub(r"a\.value =(.+?) \+ .+?;", r"\1", builder)
            builder = re.sub(r"\s{3,}[a-z](?: = |\.).+", "", builder)
            #print(builder)

        except AttributeError:
            # Something is wrong with the page. This may indicate Cloudflare has changed their
            # anti-bot technique. If you see this and are running the latest version,
            # please open a GitHub issue so I can update the code accordingly.
            raise IOError("Unable to parse Cloudflare anti-bots page. Try upgrading cfscrape, or "
                          "submit a bug report if you are running the latest version.")

        # Lock must be added explicitly, because PyV8 bypasses the GIL
        with PyV8.JSLocker():
            with PyV8.JSContext() as ctxt:
                # Safely evaluate the Javascript expression
                answer = str(int(ctxt.eval(builder)) + len(domain))

        params = {"jschl_vc": challenge, "jschl_answer": answer}
        submit_url = "%s://%s/cdn-cgi/l/chk_jschl" % (parsed.scheme, domain)
        headers["Referer"] = url

        return requests.get(submit_url, params=params, headers=headers, **kwargs)


def create_scraper(session=None):
    """
    Convenience function for creating a ready-to-go requests.Session object.
    You may optionally pass in an existing Session to mount the CloudflareAdapter to it.
    """
    sess = session or requests.session()
    adapter = CloudflareAdapter()
    sess.mount("http://", adapter)
    sess.mount("https://", adapter)
    return sess
```

I make a vote request generator ising the linked-to script. That was a big mistake The rate of WTF-moments is dangerously high; the format is outright insane. Dumping the generator here to save the sanity of someone else trying to do this. Tested with simple Accept/Reject and Yes/No/Maybe voting options:

```python
padding = '\x00\x00\x00'


def pad(bytes_seq):
    return padding + padding.join([chr(i) for i in bytes_seq]) + padding

approve_reject_header = '\x02\x01\x06\x00' + pad([])
yes_no_header = '\x02\x01\x07\x00' + pad([])
pre_footer = pad([0, 2])
post_header = '\x05\x00' + pad([0])
post_option = '\xFF\xFF\xFF\xFF'

reply_to_all_header = '\x05Reply\x08IPM.Note\x07Message\x02RE' + post_header
reply_to_all_footer = pre_footer + 'f' + pad([2, 1])
reply_to_header = '\x0cReply to All\x08IPM.Note\x07Message\x02RE' + post_header
reply_to_footer = pre_footer + 'g' + pad([3, 2])
forward_header = '\x07Forward\x08IPM.Note\x07Message\x02FW' + post_header
forward_footer = pre_footer + 'h' + pad([4, 3])
reply_to_folder_header = '\x0fReply to Folder\x08IPM.Post\x04Post\x00' + post_header
reply_to_folder_footer = pre_footer + 'l' + pad([8])
options_extra_header = '\x04\x01\x05'
options_exra_preamble = [
    'Reply\x02', 'RE\x0c', 'Reply to All\x02', 'RE\x07', 'Forward\x02', 'FW\x0f', 'Reply to Folder\x00'
]

options_id_map = {
    'Approve': 7,
    'Reject': 6,
    'Yes': 3,
    'No': 2,
    'Maybe': 5,
}

bin_true = '\x00'
bin_false = '\x01'


def encode_bool(val):
    return bin_true if val else bin_false


def encode_reply_all(v):
    return reply_to_all_header + encode_bool(not v) + reply_to_all_footer


def encode_reply_to(v):
    return reply_to_header + encode_bool(not v) + reply_to_footer


def encode_forward(v):
    return forward_header + encode_bool(not v) + forward_footer


def encode_reply_to_folder(v):
    return reply_to_folder_header + encode_bool(not v) + reply_to_folder_footer


def encode_option(order_id, option_id, option):
    return '\x04' + padding + \
           '{hex_id}{option}\x08IPM.Note\x00{hex_id}{option}\x00\x00'.format(hex_id=chr(option_id), option=option) \
           + pad([0, 1, 2, 2, order_id]) + post_option


def encode_options_extra(options):
    encoded_options = ''
    for option in options:
        encoded_options += '{hex_id}{option}{hex_id}{option}'.format(
            hex_id=chr(options_id_map[option]),
            option=''.join('%s\x00' % s for s in option)
        )
    encoded_options_exra_preamble = ''.join('\x00'.join(s) for s in options_exra_preamble)
    return options_extra_header + encoded_options_exra_preamble + encoded_options


def build_verbstream(options, reply_all=True, reply_to=True, forward=True, reply_to_folder=True):
    # Assemble the final verb stream. The optional args are used to encode whether this option 
    # should be enabled on the receiving end.
    header = approve_reject_header if 'Approve' in options else yes_no_header
    encoded_options = ''
    for order_id, option in enumerate(options, start=1):
        encoded_options += encode_option(order_id, options_id_map[option], option)
    verb = header \
           + encode_reply_all(reply_all) \
           + encode_reply_to(reply_to) \
           + encode_forward(forward) \
           + encode_reply_to_folder(reply_to_folder) \
           + encoded_options \
           + encode_options_extra(options)
    return verb.encode('latin1')
```

Okay, the initial question made me think rgb stored as a string rgb(1,2,3) rather than a tuple (1,2,3).

Migrating to a new jQuery color picker will likely cause some major functionality changes that may be unwanted. The current picker displays a set of colors to be chosen, whereas other pickers I've found provide a palette of colors to choose from. So no input of a colors list would be needed.

Also, CSS expects color formats as strings so sticking to that format seems like an easier option if the color field value is used on the frontend. http://www.w3schools.com/cssref/css_colors_legal.asp

In either case it seems like choosing a JS color picker that supports the wanted color formats is a better option than converting the format on the backend. However, if a color list is wanted the picker I suggested above is not a good option.

```python
prices = client.get_ticker_price("GOOGL", frequency="weekly",
```

This was done very quickly so please pardon the quality and the unsorted data.  

What I can see from the results is that there is definitely a way to use parasail incorrectly.  I published a paper describing how the 'striped' vectorization approach -- what SSW uses -- becomes very slow for global alignment.  The 'scan' vectorization approach is better for global alignment.

Looks like I have some work to do to make parasail faster.  I doubt the issue is the Python wrapper since the most it does for conversions is if you're using Python3 and need to convert unicode to bytes.  Your example code is as close to a direct comparison as far as I can tell.  The ssw code is creating both the 8-bit and 16-bit profiles and then running the 8-bit alignment function.  Although it's not a direct comparison, the 8-bit local scan or striped parasail routines are pretty close in performance even though they aren't doing quite the same steps.  I'll explain.

The biggest difference between parasail's traceback capability and that of ssw is that ssw performs two quick alignments to get the starting and ending locations and then performs a banded alignment  based on those locations to get the traceback.  Parasail calculates and stores the entire traceback array for the entire alignment.  This is not ideal, I know, I'm sorry.  I already have reports of it using a lot of memory and not running as fast.  There is definitely room for improvement.

I hope this helps you use parasail-python.  I'll create some issues about improving performance.

To fix, set use_sprint_value to false, and if your return contains binary (usually for MAC addresses), run the return through a function like this:

def mac_bin_to_hex(inc_bin_mac_address):
  octets = [ord(c) for c in inc_bin_mac_address]
  return "{:02X}:{:02X}:{:02X}:{:02X}:{:02X}:{:02X}".format(*octets)

This works for me. It’s not as elegant as fixing the library, but C is not my strong suit.

-Ryan

It's not a problem with a custom fields of an issue. ``project.issues`` returns only issues with an ``open`` status, i.e. you just don't receive issues with ``closed`` status at all using ``project.issues``. This is the default behaviour of Redmine API and Python Redmine just respects it.

So if you want to get all issues for a project, currently the only way to do it is like this:

```python
project = red.project.get('project')
issues = red.issue.filter(project_id=project.id, status_id='*')
```

A quick play with it shows that it's actually the Effects that are using the CPU, not the drawing to the Screen, so the only way to reduce CPU usage is to stop even attempting to update the Effects.  This is a trade-off that isn't going to work for everyone...  Some people will just want the original animation speed and others (like you) will want a more CPU-efficient refresh rate.

I've tried to make the UI more responsive whenever there is any input, but to fall back to a configurable idle frame rate with this patch (https://github.com/peterbrittain/asciimatics/commit/bc77670451e02a3234ed08cff648180938ea4cf6).  On my system, that reduces the CPU usage on contact_list by ~80% and yet stays just about responsive enough for human input.

Just pass `None` or an empty string for `database_path` to enable the in-memory queue.
I made this a bit clearer in https://github.com/eht16/python-logstash-async/commit/0c467c6d1b9f47eaca632233575eb51928406fff.

After thinking a bit more about it, your lags might result from https://github.com/eht16/python-logstash-async/blob/master/logstash_async/database.py#L115: here all events are fetched and their status is updated to mark them as 'currently being processed' though we need to issue multiple queries if there are many events as with Sqlite there is a limit of 1000 values you can use within a single query.
To honor this limit, I added chunked updating with a chunk size of 750 events per update.
In your case, it might be that issuing the multiple update statements to the database is the bottleneck and during their execution more new events are received and so processing gets too slow.

If this assumption is true, using the in-memory queue shoud immediately solve the problems. I'm not sure whether the Sqlite limitation can be workarounded or even solved in a more clever way.
Maybe even using Sqlite as a "buffer" isn't suitable at all for that rate of events.

It was a bit more complicated. The thread which gets LogRecords from the queue, did an eternally blocking get so when the MultiprocessingLogger was closed, it would not realize it should stop unless it received a LogRecord.
The 5s timeout is from [here](https://github.com/jruere/multiprocessing-logging/blob/master/multiprocessing_logging.py#L98), where it waits for the thread to join.

I have introduced a timeout on the get so that it loops every 200ms.
Now the maximum delay is still 5s but only if you have many messages to log. This is a good thing.
The maximum wait when there are no messages is 200ms. I hope it's good enough.

Are your clients websocket or long-polling? When a websocket client refreshes the page, there is an immediate disconnection notification on the server side, but long-polling does not work that way because there isn't a permanent connection. For long-polling, the server declares a client disconnected after a minute passes without receiving any requests.

The idea of this query is to group the terms by "slug" and bring forth the latest entry for each slug.

The result is a list with `slug` and `date_active` values of all currently active terms:
```
active_terms = [
    ('slug-1', datetime.datetime(2017, 4, 18, 10, 40, 8, 581046, tzinfo=<UTC>),
    ('slug-2', datetime.datetime(2017, 9, 18, 10, 43, 8, 581046, tzinfo=<UTC>),
]
```
The same query, can be filtered based on the user:
`user_agreed_terms = active_terms.filter(users=request.user)`

Now in `user_agreed_terms` are stored the latest terms agreed by the user.

If those sets are equal, inarguably, the user has agreed to all recent terms. If not, the difference of those sets will give the terms that the user has not agreed. Such a result could be the following:

```
pending_terms = {
    ('slug-2', datetime.datetime(2017, 9, 18, 10, 43, 8, 581046, tzinfo=<UTC>),
}
````

From this point, the slug is sent as before. This line in the old middleware fetches the first slug as `term`:

`for term in TermsAndConditions.get_active_list():`

I cannot imagine why the rest of the code favors the choice of which slug has to be sent based on the order of the slugs. 

I saw the failures but it seemed that it received a uri instead of a url in some python versions. 

I have not changed anything except from the way the slugs of the not accepted terms is fetched. The whole system underneath, expects a `slug` and receives it. I am pretty sure that the whole `for loop` is never executed, only the first loop. A `pending_terms[0][0]` would also be sufficient because after accepting a term, the middleware runs again.

The `order_by` came forth from [this question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/46898464/django-query-group-by-field-and-get-the-latest-entry-per-group) and it has to do with this [Django detail](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/topics/db/aggregation/#interaction-with-default-ordering-or-order-by). Otherwise, the results of the query are not correct.

Since with_alias is an instance method of Table, you already have the DAL object (self._db), so all you need to check is whether the cached alias in that DAL object refers to the same dalname - which is trivial, as proven by my workaround.

It only ever becomes complicated when you abandon alias caching, because then indeed you would need to compare the entire object hierarchy - so that is why it seems absurd to do that.

It would make a lot more sense to move the alias caching into the Table object itself, so that you can skip the dalname check and only ever look at the alias. Then, whenever you call Table.with_alias with an alias name for which there already is a cached instance in /that table/, then return that same instance.

Your proposal is still for us to rewrite our code rather than fixing this newly introduced issue in PyDAL? This isn't really the most helpful advice, but if you say there is no other way and you're not going to fix this, then okay, thanks for your time.

use JSON as the configuration language.  I chose JSON for the reporter file because in the long run I'd like the different ports to be able to standardize and share configurations with each other.  

I think in the past I've solved the "where" problem by looking in the test directory, then start climbing up the tree until i found the config.  This has the advantage of allowing custom config for certain test suites and a global config for the project, or even multiple projects if you allow the search to climb out side the project's own folders.

The most common configuration options are: Where to put the approval files.  What reporter to use.  Whether to consider differences in line endings (`\n` vs `\r\n`) as actual differences.  Javascript readme was pretty good documentation of its options.

Thanks for the example. Unfortunately definition in `gluon.model_zoo.vision` is slightly different from symbol model zoo (example/image-classification), which would break compatibility of all released models and thus require re-train them all.

To preserve the network definition, we could translate the current symbol definition to `gluon.Block` and get a symbol as you suggested. However, the result will not bear connection to `gluon.model_zoo` and will lose connection to the symbol model zoo as it has now.

As mentioned above, `dmlc/gluon-cv` uses gluon model zoo and trains model in gluon API. So I think the better way is to preserve the symbol definition like example/image-classification and link to the pure gluon API implementation `dmlc/gluon-cv`.

```
sphinx-build -b html -d _build/doctrees   . _build/html
Running Sphinx v1.7.6

Configuration error:
There is a programable error in your configuration file:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/taylor/.local/share/virtualenvs/tmp-afb06f74a17a352/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sphinx/config.py", line 161, in __init__
    execfile_(filename, config)
  File "/Users/taylor/.local/share/virtualenvs/tmp-afb06f74a17a352/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sphinx/util/pycompat.py", line 150, in execfile_
    exec_(code, _globals)
  File "conf.py", line 16, in <module>
    import mock
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'mock'

make: *** [html] Error 2
```

This happens because conf.py for Sphinx [imports mock on line 16](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/1b4961a0c5ebf5e314130a3fc97fb06fcb7c0c1a/docs/conf.py#L16) which it uses to build `MOCK_MODULES`.  That appears to be [a Sphinx pattern for a build system not having some module dependencies](http://docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html#i-get-import-errors-on-libraries-that-depend-on-c-modules), which given the modules in that list, makes sense to me in a limited environment where I'm not doing a big install with more extras.  Adding the mock change that's in this PR resolves that issue.

2\. The CI issue fix... so if I try to replicate the 4 extras that I believe RTD is installing per .readthedocs.yml, with `mock` removed from the `docs` extra, then run the build script which effectively runs `make html` which effectively runs `sphinx-build -b html . _build/html`, I get the same error.

```
$ READTHEDOCS=True pip install -e .[doc,docker,gcp_api,emr]
$ cd docs/
$ ./build.sh
...same error as above...
```

This makes me think there might be something wonky with RTD.  Perhaps it's running a different command than `make html`, or somehow is getting other extras installed that include mock (e.g., any of the extras that build on `devel` in setup.py).  As far as I can tell [from the RTD docs](http://docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/builds.html#understanding-what-s-going-on) they are running the same command that errors out for me outside of their environment when I don't supply the mock dependency.

> When we build your documentation, we run sphinx-build -b html . _build/html, where html would be replaced with the correct backend. 

I'm all for getting to the bottom of the mock issue, but I also know that getting the docs site updating again is probably more urgent.  If not having mock doesn't seem to affect RTD, I'm happy to remove it here.

**So the mock issue is really a completely separate issue from the GPL RTD issue.  Should I separate it from this PR?  Or keep it here and add a comment?  What do you think?**

P.S. I also fixed some trailing whitespace in .readthedocs.yml.

I found the solution now.
It seem like the way i init the zmq context the wrong way.

I checked the source code
```python

cdef class Context:

    cdef object __weakref__     # enable weakref
    cdef void *handle           # The C handle for the underlying zmq object.
    cdef bint _shadow           # whether the Context is a shadow wrapper of another
    cdef void **_sockets        # A C-array containg socket handles
    cdef size_t _n_sockets      # the number of sockets
    cdef size_t _max_sockets    # the size of the _sockets array
    cdef int _pid               # the pid of the process which created me (for fork safety)
    
    cdef public bint closed   # bool property for a closed context.
    cdef inline int _term(self)
    # helpers for events on _sockets in Socket.__cinit__()/close()
    cdef inline void _add_socket(self, void* handle)
    cdef inline void _remove_socket(self, void* handle)
```

the context object hold all the socket handles, but i create  the context every time i create the zmq_socket. So the context should be a global variable.So i just init the context in global way , the problem solved.

I'm not familiar with tox, but the my understanding is that in the following configuration `py26` doesn't refer to the python version but to the tox environment. Since we are using travis to manage python version, there isn't any tox environment matching py26.

```ini
[testenv]
deps=
  py26: unittest2
```

To fix this issue, I added unittest2 to requirement and use unitest2 only when the corresponding function doesn't exist in unittest

```python
try:
    from unittest import skipIf

    from unittest2 import skipIf
```

Putting the aggregation on the user side would actually be fairly difficult for me. Consider the work needed to convert a job that does daily overwrites to use max batching. Under my scheme, you just add `batch_method=MAX` to the date parameter. Under your method, you set `batch=True`, change the parameter class to accept a list, tweak your run function a little, scour your code base for any instances of `obj.date` and make sure you change those, scour your code base for any time you construct one of these and change those, realize you have some places where max batched and non batched jobs are mixed so you'll have to convert all your date parameters to use lists, and so on.

It's also less than ideal in the scheduler, as you're getting a much longer than necessary job description. If you have an hourly job that's behind a week, we could be talking about hundreds of parameters vs just one. I'm not sure whether this will break anything as the description of the job gets too large. In cases where you actually need the full list, you'll probably want to add a batch size limit which will prevent things from getting out of control. With a max parameter, you definitely don't want that.

Most weird cases should be handled in the way you describe, but I think having MAX and LIST is a good compromise between the complexity added to the code base usability.

Android compilation is a bit troublesome.

If you're on Windows, you can compile Assimp using these:

https://ninja-build.org/ <-Ninja for building
https://github.com/taka-no-me/android-cmake/blob/master/android.toolchain.cmake <-Toolchain for Android compilation
https://cmake.org/download/ <-CMake for generating projects
https://developer.android.com/studio/index.html <-Android SDK and NDK (required, you will also need to download the minimum Assimp Android API level, which is level 9)

Sorry for not posting the details, as I'm a little busy now, but basically, you will:
*Install the SDK and NDK (that are downloadable from Android Studio)
*Thats important to check if all Android SDK envoriment variables are configured (here I have: ANDROID_NDK, JAVA_HOME, JDK_ROOT, NDK_HOME, NDKROOT)
*Extract Android CMake Toolchain to any folder you want
*Extract CMake-GUI, and preferentially, extract Ninja at the same folder
*Thats important to check if your system can find Ninja via envoriment variables. You should have your PATH variable pointing to Ninja folder
*Start CMake-GUI, point the input folder to the folder where you extracted Assimp source
*Set the output folder to any folder where you want the project to be buil
*Click on "Configure", and at the settings, select "Ninja Makefiles" specifying the toolchain
*Select your extracted toolchain path in the textbox
*If everything is ok, CMake-GUI will expand all Assimp compilation options, you can leave them that way, only disabling STL importer, as it´s not compilable for Android afaik
*By clicking "Generate", CMake-GUI will generate the project at the folder you've previously selected
*Start a Windows CMD session at the project folder (Shift+Right click on Explorer)
*Type "Ninja" and press ENTER
*If everything is ok, Ninja will build "libassimp.so" inside project "Code" folder

> if I have an asset with this kind of "common" material, how can I (today) export that to glTF2 with assimp and successfully load it with loaders that are supporting a WIP spec?

You probably cannot do that today, and there should be no expectation that you can. For all intents and purposes, `KHR_materials_common` does not exist today. It may exist in a few weeks/months, or it may never exist. `KHR_materials_cmnBlinnPhong` may end up replace it, or it may also never come to fruition.

Besides the point, assimp currently doesn't export `KHR_materials_common` properly against any of the WIP specs.

The only argument for keeping in (some form of) KHR_materials_common is that three.js supports it, but that's not a good argument. Especially since a few weeks ago they removed another extension (Lights, I believe) since its spec was incomplete. I can see three.js's implementation of `KHR_materials_common` being changed in the future.

There's no sense in generating glTF2 files that are, plain and simple, invalid.

It looks like that is the only way to get get that value. Furthermore, I do not think we can add it as an attribute of the VPC class because it is unavailable in the the classes loader [operation](https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.Client.describe_vpcs) nor its shape. Adding a customization that adds that attribute would also be tricky because it would be hard to do a customization that has the notion of loading a value for a particular attribute with a different loader.

What we could do though is expose a ``VpcAttribute`` class and make that a subresource of VPC. So the workflow would look something like this which is still really verbose:
```py
Vpc('vpc-aaaaaaaa').Attribute('enableDnsHostnames').enable_dns_host_names.value
```

I am not sure if there is a great way for shortening this. If EC2 exposed that attribute in DescribeVPCs, boto3 would pick up the attribute immediately, which would be the best option. It would not hurt to ask for this as a feature request in the forums: https://forums.aws.amazon.com/forum.jspa?forumID=30.

Let us know what you think.

I think it's been discussed above and elsewhere, but there is a tension/tradeoff in the design space between npm/yarn and pipenv. Any package manager ostensibly has these goals, with some relative priority:

- Make it easy to install and upgrade packages
- Make it hard to accidentally break your app with an errant dependency upgrade

The trouble with pinning an exact version in the Pipfile is that it's then harder to upgrade packages; this is why [pip-tools](https://github.com/jazzband/pip-tools/) exists (though it's for requirements.txt).

Setuptools and pip are filtered out intentionally because they are essential for installing packages, and not considered part of the reproducible environment setup. `pip freeze`, which is also for managing reproducible environment, has a similar behaviour. Those two packages are not only excluded for `--outdated`, but for almost all operations in Pipenv.

@techalchemy Breaking out low level building blocks to make them easier to compose independently seems like a good idea to me. (As one example: it would be cool if `pip-compile` could accept `Pipfile` as input directly, and this would be a useful stepping stone towards enabling that)

@ksamuel The problem is not that Pipenv does not work with Python 2.7, but that it can have problem if you run it on non-matching Python versions to your virtualenv. Even if Pipenv is Python 3-only (I think it should, but that’s another topic altogether), it would still have problems working with virtualenvs not matching your virtualenvs.

The only possible thing Pipenv can do right now is 3., i.e. provide better error reporting. It is still much more difficult than you might imagine, however, since `setup.py` files can contain arbitrary Python code, and those out there certainly do contain all sorts of code that can fail with all kinds of errors in all kinds of places. It is doable, but would require work, which is lacking.

The overarching goal is that manylinux wheels should "just work". This is a somewhat fuzzy criterion. But generally for whitelisted libraries it means they need to (a) have a consistent abi across all glibc-based Linux distros that people actually use, and (b) be installed by default on all the the glibc-based based Linux distros that people actually use (at least in the configurations where they try to use your software).

Unfortunately these are somewhat difficult to check except by shipping a bunch of software and then waiting to see if people complain. The whitelist in PEP 513 was generated by empirically looking at what anaconda and canopy were doing. So it's possible that Xt should be on the whitelist but it just happens that no one noticed; or it could be that they tried using the system Xt at some point but it caused problems so they stopped.

@cournape might happen to remember. You should also check whether it's included in a default Ubuntu desktop install; that's a quick check that might rule this out quickly.

And of course the other thing to consider is, is having libXt in .libs causing any problems? Is this issue one that you care enough about to spend energy on?

If after considering all that you still think it should be whitelisted, then here's what I suggest: somehow force it to be whitelisted locally just for your builds, ship some wheels that don't include it, and then if in 6 months or whatever no one has complained, you can come back to us like "I told you so" and we'll add it to the global whitelist. 

The problem with adding it to the global whitelist preemptively before gathering data is then we might be breaking random other projects that were working just fine until we started suddenly using them as guinea pigs without their knowledge. And without our knowledge either, for that matter...

This does mean that you'd be shipping wheels that technically weren't PEP 513 compliant, but whatever, [it's a PEP, not a cop](https://m.imgur.com/gallery/mSHi8). It's not like pypi validates manylinux wheels for compliance or anything.

On the auditwheel side, this suggests that we might want a convenient way to override the policy file from the command line, in order to run experiments like this? Of course it's possible to override by locally hacking the source and that's certainly doable, but it's nicer not to have to do that.

It's tricky because the 'executable' argument that install_scripts pulls from build_scripts can be either
1. a single command with spaces in its path (reasonably likely on Windows, not supported on Unix)
2. or a command plus arguments (possible on both).
And there's no way to tell which it is. The current behaviour always assumes 2, this PR right now always assumes 1 which breaks issue #188 again.

Something has to give. My preference would be 1 on Windows, 2 everywhere else; that makes it impossible to specify extra arguments on Windows, but Python installed in Program Files is more useful IMHO.

Sorry, I was looking for an email re virtualenv and saw you had sent me email about this rewrite a couple of months ago re Jython. In principle, this sounds great. It currently doesn't work on Jython at this time however. Please note a couple of things:

* Jython has historically used `JYTHONPATH`, not `PYTHONPATH`. But there's only one dependency in the code, so should be easy to fix.
* Jython now supports ensurepip, with corresponding pip and setuptools wheels bundled in. However, we have a patched version of pip that we use, https://github.com/jythontools/pip, which of course needs to be upstreamed at some point.

I've updated `distlib` to handle long paths and paths with spaces. I didn't use Harald Nordgren's patch directly (it had some problems - e.g. no tests) but the approach is the same - using '/bin/sh' as the executable. `pip` / `virtualenv` maintainers might want to test after locally vendoring the current version of the `distlib` repository.
I'd guess it's the "filename = os.path.normcase(os.path.abspath(filename))" line added in ac4ea65b14270caeac56b1e1e64c56928037ebe2.

I'm not familiar with virtualenv's internal workings but my understanding is that for "updating" a venv to a newer version of Python would require virtualenv to do some other work (like making sure all the packages are compatible w/ the newer version). I'm not sure that it does all that.

FWIW, when I try to upgrade, I delete the existing virtualenv and make a new virtualenv (with the newer Python) in the same directory and re-setup my environment by installing the requisite packages. If you need to have access to multiple python versions, I'd suggest you use a tool that helps deal with multiple virtualenvs for a project, like [nox](https://pypi.org/project/nox-automation/) or [tox](https://pypi.org/project/tox/).

I don't think we need to go as far as trying to model everything that Spark is providing (like a context object that you can play with in a Python REPL).  The infrastructure of Storm is too different from Spark to allow for that sort of experimentation where you take some data and apply a bunch of functions to it that magically get executed on the cluster.  With Storm your tuples are always coming out of a spout and then just getting fired through the topology.

Anyway, I really like the idea of just requiring that people have Storm installed.  It simplifies a lot of things.  For example, we could just reuse `storm shell` to submit our Python DSL topologies if we did (although we would probably want to provide a less verbose wrapper around it).

@albalu @aykol @xhqu1981 

All - this thread is getting ridiculous.

Here is our problem, simply stated:

We have a Firework with 4 Firetasks:
- copy output from a previous run (OUTCAR and vasprun.xml)
- use that information to generate inputs (INCAR, POSCAR, KPOINTS, POTCAR) for the current run
- use custodian to run VASP
- parse the resulting output files and put them in the database

What we have found is that, in many instances, this Firework looks to be COMPLETED successfully. It is listed as COMPLETED in the Fireworks database, and in the tasks database there is indeed a new entry (meaning all 4 Firetasks did in fact complete). So far, nothing appears to be the problem.

But, upon closer inspection of the directory, what has actually happened is that step 3 (use custodian to run VASP) never actually executed correctly. During this part, when calling the mpirun command, there was some error returned from NERSC/SLURM/whatever and thus vasp never actually started. This is almost certainly due to some temporary issue that happened at NERSC. Unfortunately, because step 3 never throws an error in such cases, step 4 continues as normal - parsing the outputs (which are actually copied from the previous run, i.e., from step 1) and inserting those previous outputs in the database. The resulting database entry is for the previous run, and uses all the parameters/outputs of the previous run. Furthermore, downstream Fireworks still run in this instance (since the current Firework is COMPLETED) but then fail.

Ideally, we would like for the chain of execution to stop as soon as there is a failure, i.e., when the mpirun command cannot complete successfully. This will prevent problems like incorrect database entries being inserted as well as downstream Fireworks running. *We would like for custodian to check the returncode from the run, and if it is not zero, to throw an error (or at least have an option to enable this).* If this were implemented, the errors would be extremely easy to debug and fix. As it stands, with bad database entries and downstream Fireworks failing for strange reasons, this is not the case.

Alireza and I have also talked about alternate solutions that don't involve custodian - e.g., such as deleting the output files of the old run from the current directory once we are done using them to generate input files. We have also discussed other validation enhancements such as having the MatMethods Vaspdrone check for more output files (e.g., EIGENVAL, IBZKPT) rather than just the OUTCAR/vasprun.xml - this would also solve our specific problem. But the best solution is if custodian throws an error when executing a job shell command and getting a non-zero error code.

Finally, don't worry about the size of the sparse matrix. That is not the responsibility of this algorithm. Many, many functions in scikit-image (and scipy) will run out of memory for large 3D images. (Or 2D for that matter — plenty of scientific instruments produce multi-gigapixel images.) We just provide the building blocks, and users can devise their own chunking approaches to make the problem tractable. See e.g. dask.array. The only responsibility is not to use a *stupidly inefficient* matrix, e.g. a dense matrix. =)
