@PrimozGodec Use `matplotlib.pyplot.imshow` to display an image, not the `Image` or `Pillow` library. 

```python
import numpy as np
from skimage import io, color
from skimage.transform import resize
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rgb = io.imread("../../small_dataset/" + file)
rgb = resize(rgb, (256, 256))
plt.imshow(rgb)
```

The problem is that the order of pixels/channels expected by `Image` is different from the order expected by scikit-image/matplotlib. See here for more info:

http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/numpy_images.html

Please feel free to comment on this issue if you have further questions!

@PuppySaturation - Generally speaking, if the tests are bad at actually evaluating the function _feel completely free_ to improve them. Trivial tests are easy to write, but aren't good long-term.

I'm completely fine with hardcoded values, if there is a known valid example available or if one can hand-calculate the expected results. This type of test is very powerful, even if seemingly rather simple. Note that for this type of test, `np.testing.assert_allclose` is better than checking exact equality as future alternative implementations may well have slightly different, but effectively equal floating point results.

@mmargenot  I would consider your issue different from the one reported by @CaptainKanuk. The problem you faced is due to pd.qcut (the function that calculate the quantiles) not being able to provide a workaround when identical values span more than one quantile. Interesting to note, in pandas 0.20.0 they added an option (duplicates='raise'|'drop') that fixes that, which would result in less bins than specified, and some larger bins (with more elements) than others. We could make use of that option eventually.

Until then, another workaround could be to use bins=5 and quantiles=None, that chooses the bins/quantiles to be evenly spaced according to the values themselves, while quantiles=5 chooses the bins/quantiles so that you have the same number of records in each bin/quantile.

And this is my custom client (in case it matters):

```python
class RavenClient(DjangoClient):
    """
    Adds the logging request_uuid identifier to the exception sent to
    Sentry, it enables finding all logs related to a given exception.
    """

    def build_msg(self, *args, **kwargs):
        data = super().build_msg(*args, **kwargs)

        log_data = getattr(threadlocal, 'log_data', None)

        if log_data and 'request_uuid' in log_data:
            data['extra']['request_uuid'] = log_data['request_uuid']

        return data
```

It seems this problem is outside the scope of Patroni.

As for the tests, I think they actually uncover an issue with the code. When the replica_method is set to base backup (because there is no specific configuration in the tests), it tries to call the base backup in line 216 of postgresql.py, returning non-zero value (as subprocess.call returns 1 for the first call), but right after that it goes into the else and re-executes the same command in line 250, returning 0, so the whole test actually returns 0.

I think here both the test and the code it covers are wrong.

And as a note, generally, our tests are more coverage tests : we don't actually run PostgreSQL, or etcd, and do not create replicas. The goal of those tests is to make sure that the code does't have obvious syntax errors. What we need are regression tests that actually start Patroni, etcd (in the docker), do actions like failover and verify the result. Do you have any experience with writing such tests in Python?

While I see nothing wrong in creating users in the `post_bootstrap` script I also see no reason why do we create users mentioned in the Patroni configuration before and not after running the post_bootstrap script.

I'd expect people would use one of those two, i.e. either do everything via the `post_bootstrap` or create users with the Patroni configuration. Mixing both looks a bit weird, you will probably end up with a script that assigns privileges to a given set of users while there is no definition of those users anywhere in the script. So creating 'normal' users before and not after the post_bootstrap phase looks like a nuance to me.

As I already explained, `urllib3==1.23` was released about a week ago. It is used in `requests` module, but the `requests` explicitly requires `urllib3<=1.22`.

From our side, I don't see any single reason to limit maximum versions for neither `requests`, nor `urllib3`, because if we go this way, we will have to update dependencies every single time when the new module is released.

We can't live with very old versions either, because our goal is to be able to run Patroni with the least possible amount of external dependencies. Ideally all dependencies should be installed from packages of your favorite distro. The latest Debian and Ubuntu actually allow this. Moreover, they provide Patroni package! 

And actually talking about weird dependency system in python. I am really surprised that nobody yet complained about `psycopg2`:
```python
>>> import psycopg2
/home/akukushkin/git/patroni/vpy31/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psycopg2/__init__.py:144: UserWarning: The psycopg2 wheel package will be renamed from release 2.8; in order to keep installing from binary please use "pip install psycopg2-binary" instead. For details see: <http://initd.org/psycopg/docs/install.html#binary-install-from-pypi>.
```
The funny thing is, `psycopg2-binary` and `psycopg2` are two different modules, but they install files in the same place on filesystem! You can even install both at the same time and pip won't complain, but overwrite existing files. If you decide to remove one of the above modules, the second one will stop working, because files are deleted...

Anyway, `requests==2.19.0` was released 6 hours ago. That means the problem is solved.

Here's the story.

VTE's C api did not change, but its Python bindings indeed did. The intended, correct, and as of vte-0.52 finally achieved version is where there is _no_ separate parameter for the length, that is, only 2 arguments are passed to the method. The "(array length=length)" annotation is responsible for this correct binding.

The `feed()` method (irrelevant here) had it at least since 0.38 (the beginning of the current VTE API).

The `feed_child_binary()` method (still not what you have problems with) received this fix in version 0.46, silently squeezed into a bigger [commit 00f252725](https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/vte/commit/00f252725).

The `feed_child()` method, what you care about, only got this fix for version 0.52 as [commit a1cd4a4e6](https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/vte/commit/a1cd4a4e6).

Now comes the twist.

Ubuntu breaks it.

(Xenial is still okay, Y-Z-A are beyond EOL, so I only care about Bionic LTS and future releases.)

In the original Bionic release they had a faulty change (silently squeezed into their big PCRE2 patch) that erroneously removed the required annotation from all three of these methods, requiring them to take 3 parameters. In a subsequently released fix they removed this broken patch, "fixing" VTE (making it behave as upstream), but this on the other hand broke some apps that relied on the broken behavior. Therefore, in turn, they are about to reintroduce this patch for Bionic, see [Launchpad 1780501](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubuntu-release-upgrader/+bug/1780501).

As far as I understand, and I do sincerely hope, they only do this back and forth in Bionic; in Cosmic they'll hopefully settle with the correct mainstream solution and fix all the apps that relied on the faulty behavior.

What can you do for now?

- Use try-except.

- Branch on vte version, and blame Ubuntu for the remaining breakages.

- Require vte >= 0.52, use two parameters, and blame Ubuntu for the remaining breakages.

- Require vte >= 0.46, do the charset conversion manually, use feed_child_binary() with two parameters, and blame Ubuntu for the remaining breakages.

I'm sorry for any inconvenience this story caused.

So I suppose there is nothing to do, except copy/paste.
//Stefan

Explications du commit 07d865d :

J'ai poussé la logique discutée dans l'issue #368 un peu plus loin : 
Au lieu de séparer salaire_super_brut en deux variables, je la sépare en trois variables : 
- salaire_super_brut représente maintenant le salaire brut + charges employeur, soit en fait ce qui est affiché sur la fiche de paie. 
- cout_du_travail est la somme de salaire_super_brut et des exonérations (ex. ZRR) ou allègements (ex. Fillon), qui impactent donc immédiatement le coût du travail, mais n'apparaissent pas sur la fiche de paie. 
- cout_du_travail_final qui ajoute lui à cout_du_travail les aides, crédits et charges différées. Par exemple, l'aide à l'embauche du premier salarié, le CICE et le TEHR.

About augmenting data, we recently updated the catalog with `UPDATE` queries for tables, e.g: https://cartodb.github.io/bigmetadata/spain/age_gender/es.ine.t1_1/es.ine.pop_0_4.html. As a note, with some reshuffling, you can do a create table in one go (similar to what the builder analysis does internally). I guess this isn't too useful for cartoframes, since it augments existing tables, instead of creating new ones with the augmented data.

```sql
CREATE TABLE augmented_do_test_points AS (
WITH data AS (
  SELECT cartodb_id AS id, the_geom FROM do_test_points
),
meta AS (
  SELECT OBS_GetMeta(ST_SetSRID(ST_Extent(the_geom), 4326),
  '[{"numer_id": "es.ine.pop_0_4",
    "denom_id": "es.ine.t1_1",
    "normalization": "denominated"}]'
  , 1, 1) meta
FROM data),
augmentation AS (
  SELECT id AS cartodb_id, (data->0->>'value')::Numeric AS pop_0_4_2015
  FROM OBS_GetData(
      (SELECT ARRAY_AGG((the_geom, id)::geomval) FROM data),
      (SELECT meta FROM meta))
)
SELECT orig.*, augmentation.pop_0_4_2015
FROM do_test_points AS orig
LEFT JOIN augmentation USING (cartodb_id)
)


SELECT cdb_cartodbfytable('javitonino', 'augmented_do_test_points')
```

Anyway, there is still a lot of templating going on, to get column names, etc. So, some options to do this:
1. Do it in python. It involves two roundtrips to the database (GetMeta to get column names, and then GetData)
2. Do it in SQL with functions, which is the current approach. You do two SQL roundtrips, one to create the function and one to execute it.
3. Do it in SQL with a [do block](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.5/static/sql-do.html). Just avoid creating the function, and send everything in a single `do` block. You just need minimal templating (to pass in the table name and meta). 

I guess option 3 is the one that fits both sides the best, right now. I was thinking about using the analysis API's instead, but they are not ready for prime time yet. For example, they need to be used in conjunction with a map. It could be cool to use with this, as a more generic piece, but not likely to happen soon.

Bonus: for searching for columns we have three functions:
1. [`OBS_Search`](https://carto.com/docs/carto-engine/data/discovery-functions/#obssearchsearchterm). Search by name and return a summary of the columns. Quite restrictive in what it can do, and likely to be deprecated tin the future.
2. [`OBS_GetAvailableNumerators`](https://carto.com/docs/carto-engine/data/discovery-functions/#obsgetavailablenumeratorsbounds-filtertags-denomid-geomid-timespan). The current best way to do it. It doesn't have `name` filters, but you can achieve the same result with some filtering like `SELECT * FROM OBS_GetAvailableNumerators() WHERE numer_name ILIKE '%income%'`.
3. [`_OBS_GetNumerators`](https://github.com/CartoDB/observatory-extension/blob/develop/src/pg/sql/42_observatory_exploration.sql#L184). Currently is a private API for builder use only, please, don't use it for cartoframes. However, I write it here, because it has search built-in as a function parameter and some extra builder-specific features. Just mentioning it in case you see something you like in this API, maybe we can improve it and make it public.

I would recommend you to go with 2, at the moment, but we can discuss improvements for the future, if you are not launching with the discovery functions.

After 2 days' debugging, I ended up with the following conclusion. This is actually not a bug. This is caused by cuDNN's non-determinism. 

I run the following simple test: I feed the same feature maps and filter parameters into cuDNN and call forward (or backward) for multiple times, the output by different calls will be slightly different (no matter I launch DyNet multiple times or have multiple calls using a for loop within one launch). This explains why the approximated gradients of some irrelevant parameters will be non-zero sometimes (i.e. grad_check not pass.).

This is also a known issue in other communities. Fortunately, I haven't seen any report saying this will affect the statistical convergence.

My take on this is that simply focusing on the quantity of typing or the relative aesthetic elegance of the code is distracting from more important issues. To me, there are three: 1) consistency between gcloud API projections in different languages; 2) consistency between gcloud-python API and other APIs that users might use; and 3) user expectations about what should be "remembered".

**Consistency between gcloud API projects in different languages**

_Why does it matter?_ In a polyglot world, it's not unreasonable that developers have to move between languages, from client-side JavaScript to server-side front-end Python to server-side back-end Java (as just one example). If I've used the Datastore API in one language, it should feel familiar when using it in another language on another part of the stack. Further (and perhaps more importantly), when I search the web for answers to my Datastore usage questions, if I find an answer on StackOverflow that is in Ruby, I should be able to immediately adapt that answer to my code in Python. The examples from Ruby and Node that JJ listed at the top of this thread made sense to me and as a user I would be surprised if Python worked differently. If the document on cloud.google.com had a "how to connect to Datastore" inset that had tabs for each language, I would be annoyed if I were flipping back and forth between the various languages and the Python sample deviated from the norm established by the rest.

**Consistency between gcloud-python and other APIs that users might use**

_Why does it matter?_ Our users do not have access to Datastore outside of GCP (with the exception of the Datastore fake when doing local App Engine development) and they will not have experience working with Datastore until they become GCP customers. But our users *will* have experience running MySQL clients, SQLlite, CouchDB, MongoDB, and many others. And the bulk of the first-party Python APIs for those products employ the model of a client/connection object with operations hanging off that object. When I read the samples for the Datastore API that did NOT use the client/connection model, I was surprised as a user because I could not relate it to my prior experience with these other APIs. That created a cognitive load because I now needed to "figure out" how this API worked because all I knew upon first reading was that it didn't work like the others I was already familiar with. I have years of experience working with many different databases using different languages on different platforms. I want my APIs to tap into my knowledge, not set it aside.

**User expectations about what should be "remembered"**

_Why does it matter?_ As I user I rely on the fact that computers should have a better memory than I do. I put things into computers so that I don't have to remember them. I put things into computers because there is more than I could possibly remember. The point of this is that I hate it when I tell I computer something and it doesn't remember it when I expected it to. It's bad user experience. When I read an API like:

```python
from gcloud.datastore import Connection, delete
connection = Connection(...)
delete(key0, connection=connection)
delete(key1, connection=connection)
```

it feels "forgetful" to me. I've told you my connection. Why do you keep forgetting it? Why do I have to keep reminding you of it? On the other hand, though functionally equivalent, this:

```python
from gcloud import datastore
client = datastore.Client(...)
client.delete(key1)
client.delete(key2)
```

does not create the same reaction for me. I've told the client about my connection, and without having to remind it anything about my connection, I can simply tell the client what I want it to do. In a sense, I have a delegated the responsibility of remembering those details to something and now I want to give my instructions to that something to "just take care of it". The client idiom meets that expectation, while the parameterized idiom does not.

To be clear, I have read and understood the more technical points about "stackability" and the implementation considerations and I also totally get that the "forgetfulness" argument is only at play when the user is working with multiple connection/datasets. That said, I am more concerned about the user experience of this API (in the context of the universe of gcloud and other APIs it lives in). There will no doubt be users for whom multiple connections/datasets is a requirement. Their user experience should not deviate from the mainstream user experience. Their user experience should not turn into "forgetful" mode just because their business problem got a little more complex.

Thanks @theacodes ! Looks like that did the job. For everyone interested, this is what I did in the end.

```
import logging
from threading import Event

from google.cloud import pubsub_v1

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def callback(message, event):
    logger.info(message)
    event.set()


def main():
    subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()

    event = Event()

    def callback_wrapper(message):
        callback(message, event)

    future = subscriber.subscribe('subscription/path', callback=callback_wrapper)

    event.wait()
    logger.exception('Got event. Shutting down.')
    future.cancel()
    exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

> Targets with no sources at all:
> Examples of cases where a target with no sources can legitimately exist:
> ..
> But I get how in other cases, a target with no sources indicates a bug, and you'd want to flag that.

In both of those cases, a warning is more than reasonable, and probably desirable?

> The only thing worse than boilerplate is boilerplate than you then have to fine-tune... Default sources were a step in a good direction, this feels like a step back, in some ways.

Default sources are where we all agree that this warning should not trigger unless _no_ globs are matched. So if anything, I would see this warning as a _discouragement_ of manually specifying sources on your targets. IE, something that encourages people to follow the happy path of default sources.

> Let's at least decouple these two orthogonal concepts: "default sources" and "sources that are allowed to be empty".
> So that instead of checking for the former, we check for the latter, and the former is implemented with the "these sources are allowed to be empty" flag set to True, or whatever.

Yes, that is the planned implementation of #6236 (a flag on `PathGlobs` that indicates whether all globs need to match, or just any of them). Whether that flag is exposed to targets is the next question... if we can avoid adding it, I'd like to, because that flag would itself be annoying boilerplate that encourages copy-pasting rather than factoring out defaults. 

----

I really like the idea of more challenging tests that run on commit.  Here's another option for splitting up tests: we could have the existing unit tests, then integration and system tests.

Integration tests could run alongside unit tests with the sole purpose of making sure components can call each other and pass data without throwing exceptions.  For example, if we ignore perplexity and BLEU, we can train a model for 10 batches and translate 10 sentences on 1 CPU in about a second.  Here we could try to cover command line options exhaustively.

System tests where we do care about perplexity and BLEU could run on commit (and pre-commit.sh).  This could be digit reordering or even very limited natural language translation.  Here we would test specific types of models we want to perform well.

As a developer, I like unit and integration tests to immediately (<10 seconds) answer the question "Did my latest commit break anything?".  I like system tests to make sure the full system works as expected.

I am using YAML anchors without issue in the solution I specified above. I have not tried the solution proposed by @sanathkr as mine is working well as is.

Here's more detail on what I am doing. The core CloudFormation template has next to nothing in it. All it does is `AWS::Include` another like so in file `BigOnion.yaml` (or whatever else you want to call it):

```
Resources:
  Fn::Transform:
    Name: AWS::Include
    Parameters:
      Location: !Sub s3://my-s3-location-${AWS::AccountId}-${AWS::Region}/resources.yaml
``` 

Then, in `resources.yaml` (or whatever else you call it), I can use YAML to radically reduce CloudFormation templates. Here's an example of a Lambda function definition:

```
accountDelete:
  <<: *lambdaDefaults
  Properties:
    <<: *lambdaProps
    FunctionName: myLambdaFunction
```

Then, to deploy, I add the following in `package.json` to simplify the command line CloudFormation deploy step:

```
  "scripts": {
    "cf-deploy": "./bin/update-resources.sh && aws --profile MyAWSProfileName cloudformation deploy --stack-name MyStackName --template-file CloudFormation/BigOnion.yaml --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM",
```

In the referenced `bin/update-resources.sh` I have the following:

```
#!/usr/bin/env bash

# Creates the S3 deployment helper bucket if it doesn't already exist.
# Uploads BigOnion_dummyFunction.zip and resources.yaml to the bucket.

PROFILE=${1:-BigOnion}
echo "Using AWS profile ${PROFILE}"
MY_REGION=`aws --profile ${PROFILE} configure get region`
echo "AWS region is ${MY_REGION}"
ACCOUNT_ID=`aws --profile ${PROFILE} sts get-caller-identity --output text --query "Account"`
echo "AWS account id is ${ACCOUNT_ID}"
S3="bigonion-deployment-helper-${ACCOUNT_ID}-${MY_REGION}"
echo "AWS S3 is ${S3}"

aws --profile ${PROFILE} s3 mb s3://$S3
aws --profile ${PROFILE} s3 cp CloudFormation/resources.yaml s3://$S3/resources.yaml
if [ ! $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "FAILED"
  exit
fi
aws --profile ${PROFILE} s3 cp functions/dummyFunction/src/BigOnion_dummyFunction.zip s3://$S3/BigOnion_dummyFunction.zip
if [ ! $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "FAILED"
  exit
fi
```

You can probably figure out what is going on here. This script basically creates the S3 bucket if it doesn't already exist, and uploads the new/updates `resources.yaml` there.

As a result, I can edit `resources.yaml` which contains all my YAML anchor'd stuff, and when I am ready to deploy, I just type: `yarn run cf-deploy`. Here's the note in `docs_source/index.rst` about the `_options` objects:

```
Many API endpoints accept optional parameters via an `_options` object in the raw JSON. 
To improve forward compatibility as options are added, the Python functions for these 
endpoints also include an `_options` argument. However, it is recommended that you 
pass in options using the provided keyword arguments where possible.
```

To recap, the `_options` objects exist primarily for flexibility in and future-proofing the library; the options in them should already be documented under their respective keyword arguments, however.

What are the Python types of your `df_x` and `df_y` variables? They don't seem to be Numpy arrays or matrices, or Pandas `DataFrame` or `Series`. 

Anyway, below is a complete example about changing the data type of the target variable from `integer` to `double` to `boolean`:
```Python
import pandas

audit_df = pandas.read_csv("Audit.csv")

from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelBinarizer
from sklearn_pandas import DataFrameMapper
from sklearn2pmml import sklearn2pmml
from sklearn2pmml.decoration import CategoricalDomain, ContinuousDomain
from sklearn2pmml.pipeline import PMMLPipeline

mapper = DataFrameMapper(
	[([column], ContinuousDomain()) for column in ["Age", "Hours", "Income"]] +
	[([column], [CategoricalDomain(), LabelBinarizer()]) for column in ["Employment", "Education", "Marital", "Occupation", "Gender", "Deductions"]]
)
classifier = RandomForestClassifier()

pipeline = PMMLPipeline([
	("mapper", mapper),
	("classifier", classifier)
])

audit_X = audit_df
audit_y = audit_df["Adjusted"]

audit_y = audit_y.astype(int)
pipeline.fit(audit_X, audit_y)
sklearn2pmml(pipeline, "Audit-int.pmml")

audit_y = audit_y.astype(float)
pipeline.fit(audit_X, audit_y)
sklearn2pmml(pipeline, "Audit-float.pmml")

audit_y = audit_y.astype(bool)
pipeline.fit(audit_X, audit_y)
sklearn2pmml(pipeline, "Audit-bool.pmml")
```

OK, I actually got it to work. I needed to convert https://github.com/Boudewijn26/gTTS-token to python3 (no actual changes required) and add the following:

```python
    def __init__(self, device):
        # The device that the speech will be sent to                                                                    
        self.device = device
        self.token = gtts_token.Token()

    def say(self, message):
        ...
        msg = cgi.escape(message)
        tk = self.token.calculate_token(msg)
        trans_URI = "x-rincon-mp3radio://translate.google.com/translate_tts?tl=%s&q=%s&tk=%s&client=tw-ob&textlen=%d" %\
 ('en', msg, tk, len(msg))
        self.device.play_uri(trans_URI, title="Speech")
```

This was actually intentional, because otherwise there would be no way to override or remove list values, since lists would only be additive.  (You can override dict values by specifying the same key again, so deep merging dicts doesn't have the same issue.)  However, particularly with the dash list YAML syntax, it's unexpected (I've seen it surprise people several times now, including myself) and there isn't a good way to handle this use-case.

One way to handle this might be to support a custom schema setting that would indicate that a list value should be merged instead of overridden.  The plus for this approach is that the schema seems a natural place to define this.  The minuses are that it applies to the option for every layer (i.e., you couldn't merge by default but also support a layer flat-out overriding the list),  that there's no way to remove an option from a merged list, and that it only works for layer opts.  The first and second aren't issues for the package opt, because you really wouldn't want to remove a package that a lower layer depends on, but it might be a reasonable thing to do for some other list value.  For the third, layer options are the most obvious and common use-case, but there might be others, e.g. the list of tags in `metadata.yaml`.

Another approach might be to have a special `$merge` value that, if found in a list,  would merge in the values from the previous layer.  This would work for any YAML file and can be controlled on a per-layer basis, but has the potential for whatever special key we choose to conflict with a real value, and it means that the `$merge` value must always be specified or all the previous merging will be dropped.

What I reckon would be the optimal solution would be allowing to "create" the config at run-time (and not just overwrite already existing) at the point in time where you need the SAS sessesion, e.g. after a user have authenticated against another system and gotten back their SAScredentials or ticket and server-details. An example is this:

We use a scheduling software (non-SAS-product) to run our SAS-jobs. This system takes care of credentials to all systems (incl. databases etc.).

When people submit a job manually they identify themselves to this system which in return will provide them with their credentials in the other system (think delegation not much unlike Kerberos in principale) and which instance to connect to (PROD, TEST, what geografic area etc). For this to work with your approach we'll have to build something into the sascfg_personal that contains all of these combinations and then provide the username and password when we startup SAS. What I would propose is that we can build the "server dict" (identical to those in the config structure-wise) dynamically and pass that to the SASSession-object. That would allow us to change it all in the database and not having to maintain all the different configurations in a seperate file.

Does this help a bit to understand why I try to push for allowing "config creation" dynamicly during the execution instead of relying to "dead" config-files?

This is happening because the SRL model requires a verb as an additional input to the model.  We use spacy to predict POS tags for each word in the input, and spacy is not finding a verb in any of those sentences, so no `Instances` are getting created.  For some of those sentences, that's because there is no verb, and the sentence is ungrammatical.  For others, spacy just didn't recognize the verb as a verb (e.g., "tail off").

We need to make the predictor robust to cases where spacy finds no verb.  The simplest thing to do would be to return an empty dictionary here: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/0c532d50ac52bca48bf9906864daf6735d5d02de/allennlp/service/predictors/semantic_role_labeler.py#L117-L118

Okay, I feel like I figured it out.  It's a pretty strange one.

I was able to reproduce this bug with the two following conditions:

1. Creating a DB off of a `template1` DB that did not have `hstore` enabled.
1. Using the `django.contrib.gis.db.backends.postgis` backend.

If you create a django project database from a template that does not have `hstore` enabled, for some reason the `postgis` backend will not properly use `hstore` on any test database that is created and instead return the data as a string (`"prop1"=>"1", "prop2"=>"test_value"`).  I had originally thought that the test database was created only from the template database at test runtime, but this does not appear to be true.  If you enable `hstore` on the template database and the django project database used, `django-hstore` will still fail on tests if you did not originally create the django project database from a template with the `hstore` extension.  This bug does not occur with the `django.db.backends.postgresql_psycopg2` backend.  

I am still not sure why this exists but was able to reproduce the issue as shown below.  Currently, my only suggestion to resolve this issue is to drop the django project database and recreate it from a template with the `hstore` extension enabled.  Obviously, this is extremely inconvenient to do with a production database (backing up the data, renaming the db, creating a new db, loading in the data).  There is probably a better solution but, at this time, I am unsure of what that is.

Hi, thanks for the thanks, and for the bug report.

I believe the "Failed to get ACE version number." error is a bit misleading. The real problem is the last line: "No such file or directory: 'ace'". This suggests that PyDelphin is unable to find your ACE executable. 

First, ensure that ACE is installed, or at least downloaded: http://sweaglesw.org/linguistics/ace/

Second, PyDelphin assumes that `ace` is executable as a system command (on Linux that means that `ace` is in a directory listed in the environment variable `PATH`; I'm not a Mac user so I'm not certain what that translates to, but this [StackOverflow answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/16288733/1441112) might be informative).

Also, if you look further down in the tutorial, there is the [Additional Arguments](https://github.com/delph-in/pydelphin/wiki/Ace-Tutorial#additional-arguments) section. The `executable` parameter to the ace commands allow you to specify the path of the `ace` binary without modifying PATH. Try following the example in that section and replace the value of `executable` with the path to your ACE binary.

Does that help? Maybe I should update the tutorial to make that more clear...

He and @asmeurer hashed out most of the details in #1601, but if you really want to catch up, you should probably read everything in [this list](https://github.com/conda/conda/pulls?q=is%3Apr+author%3Aukoethe+is%3Aclosed).  (Have fun...)

>- `packaging` implements PEP440 and PEP508. The current versioning classes in conda seem to implement the older PEP380.

See the above-mentioned #1601 for discussion of our adherence to PEP440.  In particular, [this comment](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1601#issuecomment-140347561) and [this comment](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1601#issuecomment-142586245).  (But there is no mentioned of PEP508, as far as I can tell.)

>- code reuse is usually a good idea
>- maintained by PyPA, who also maintain pip

Indeed, these are laudable motivations.  If an externally managed library from a reliable source really does cover all of our use-cases, then I guess we must admit that the amount of previous effort that's been sunk into this issue is not so relevant (even if it would be frustrating to discard it).

>- Covers as many versioning schemes as I can think of

Of course, it is critical to keep in mind that conda supports multiple languages, and therefore has slightly different requirements than PyPA.  For instance, #1652 was implemented specifically for [compatibility with R's silly versioning scheme](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1601#issuecomment-143042338).

PS -- PyPA? I might be biased, but as far as I'm concerned, the "authority" on packaging python packages at this point is the Anaconda project.  Maybe they should be using our code, not the other way around... :-P

Not 100% sure what exactly happens here. The issue is likely the behaviour of pysam when opening. If existing file objects or file handles are passed to AlignmentFile, the file descriptor will be duplicated using fdup. This is to prevent issues with a file object going out of scope unexpectedly. Basically, the following works:
```
with open("x.bam") in inf:
     pysam_file = AlignmentFile(inf.fileno())
pysam_file.fetch()
```
Without duplication, pysam_file.fetch() would fail. Also, you would get a double close when garbage collecting both the original file object and the derived AlignmentFile.

The option "duplicate_filehandle" to AlignmentFile controls this behaviour. The following works:
```
    c = pysam.AlignmentFile(pipeR.fileno(), duplicate_filehandle=False)
    pipeR.close()
    d = pysam.AlignmentFile(sys.stdout, 'w', template=c, duplicate_filehandle=False)
```
but produces ugly ignored exceptions about truncated files. 

Possibly related is what has been described here: https://stackoverflow.com/a/7942344 
```
What happens is that when two parallel child processes get started the second child inherits the pipes of the parent, so that when the parent calls self.parent_conn.close() the second child still has that pipe file descriptor open, so that the pipe file description doesn't get closed in the kernel (the reference count is more than 0), with the effect being that self.child_conn.recv_bytes() in the first parallel child process never read()s EOF and EOFError gets never thrown.
```

Well, it's not a 504, but you are getting several 503 errors, which are "Service unavailable" errors.

```
2015-08-17 12:07:25,544 WARNING            elasticsearch       log_request_fail:82   PUT /_snapshot/my_s3_repository/mdp-2015.08.04?wait_for_completion=true [status:503 request:0.007s]
```

You're also seeing a 60 second timeout:

```
2015-08-17 12:07:25,443 WARNING            elasticsearch       log_request_fail:82   PUT http://haproxystaging:9200/_snapshot/my_s3_repository/mdp-2015.08.04?wait_for_completion=true [status:N/A request:60.002s]
```

This timeout is what the `**ProtocolError: ('Connection aborted.'` part is reporting.

Just from the hostname, `haproxystaging`, I presume you have your Elasticsearch clients behind some kind of proxy gateway.  This is why you're timing out.  Your HAProxy is forcing the client connection to timeout at 60 seconds, just like in the FAQ I referenced in my last comment.

Your options include:

1. Don't connect to the HAProxy for snapshot operations, but instead have a direct client connection.
2. Use `--wait_for_completion False` (it will run in the background, and you'll have to use some other tool to check for completion).

```
$credentials = (ConvertFrom-Json $env:AzureCliLogin)

    $tenant = $credentials.tenant
    $clientId = $credentials.appId
    $clientSecret = $credentials.password
    $subscriptionId = "<subscriptionid>"

    $body = @{
        "grant_type"="client_credentials";
        "client_id"=$clientId;
        "client_secret"=$clientSecret;
        "resource"="https://management.azure.com/"
    }

    $authInfo = Invoke-RestMethod -Uri "https://login.microsoftonline.com/$tenant/oauth2/token" -Body $body -Method Post -Headers @{"Content-Type"="application/x-www-form-urlencoded"} 

    $publishData = Invoke-RestMethod -Uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$subscriptionId/resourceGroups/$resourceGroup/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/$name/publishxml?api-version=2016-08-01" -Method Post -Headers @{"Authorization"="Bearer $($authInfo.access_token)"}

    $userName = $publishData.publishData.publishProfile[0].userName
    $password = $publishData.publishData.publishProfile[0].userPWD

    $apiBaseUrl = "https://$name.scm.azurewebsites.net/api"
    $siteBaseUrl = "https://$name.azurewebsites.net"
    
    # For authenticating to Kudu
    $base64AuthInfo = [Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::ASCII.GetBytes(("{0}:{1}" -f $username,$password)))    
    
    # Call Kudu /api/functions/admin/token to get a JWT that can be used with the Functions Key API 
    $jwt = Invoke-RestMethod -Uri "$apiBaseUrl/functions/admin/token" -Headers @{Authorization=("Basic {0}" -f $base64AuthInfo)} -Method GET
    
    # Call Functions Key API to get the master key 
    $x = Invoke-RestMethod -Uri "$siteBaseUrl/admin/host/systemkeys/_master" -Headers @{Authorization=("Bearer {0}" -f $jwt)} -Method GET
    
    $masterKey = $x.value
```

You can use `az configure --defaults group=<name>` to configure a default group. Later you can clear it up with `''`. There are a few other configurable names available, like `location`, `vm`, `vmss`, `web`. 
You can also use env variable of `AZURE_DEFAULTS_GROUP`, because CLI has integration between env vars and config file through the mapping of `AZURE_<SECTION>_<VAR>`
The PR's main goal was to introduce a general mechanism, so new arguments can be configured easily moving forward. 
(For reference)
 ```bash
C:\SDK\azure-cli>az configure -h
...
    --defaults : Space separated 'name=value' pairs for common arguments defaults, e.g. '--defaults
                 group=myRG web=myweb vm=myvm'. Use '' to clear the defaults, e.g. --defaults vm=''
                 web=''.

I am afraid that the state property does not reflect the functional state of the webapp. To repro, 

1.  start a containerized webapp. 
2.  Note that state==running
3.  In a new terminal, write an infinite loop that detects when the state property changes
```
while true; do
   az webapp show --name [appname] --query "state" | awk '!/Running/'
done
```
3.  Now call az webapp restart --name [appname] in the original terminal.

The state property never changes even though the site is unresponsive for ~1-2 minutes.

It looks like you may be able to do tracing relatively easily, see http://stackoverflow.com/questions/10588644/how-can-i-see-the-entire-http-request-thats-being-sent-by-my-python-application

Service bus team used to point people to the Apache Proton library, as service bus supports AMQP 1.0, for better performance.  Not sure about now.  The links at the bottom of the page are out of date.  https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-amqp-overview

@asvetlov, what about body validation? What is the best way to implement middleware for validating request body, for example RAML (i use it in several projects).

It is necessary to check if resource url and http method is present in RAML and request body. Request body can be read only once - it's a stream. 

My idea is to:
- implement middleware that would validate resource url + http method against RAML
- decorate method `Request.json()` with validation method, that would check Request body stream against RAML and raise exception in case it is invalid or provide custom method.

I do not want users to write extra code for each handler for call against RAML validation. I would like to have DRY setup for such validation in one place.

Another idea - to read body stream from request and store it in request['body'] key seems to be far from ideal. It would be not flexible enough. 

What do you think?

Ok I figured it out. The problem was that somewhere in the sequence of imports there was an `import pdb` (from the stdlib) which in turn imported:

import os
import re
import sys
import cmd
import bdb
import dis
import code
import glob
import pprint
import signal
import inspect
import traceback
import linecache

And obviously based on the python module lookup system its going to look for `code.py` in `.` before looking in your site packages. So yes I was able to reproduce the same problem if I had `code.py`, `cmd.py`, `dis.py` etc in my working directory.

Great to see movement on this! I was just talking to @jdfreder about resurrecting the cell tagging efforts.

UI features that aren't strictly related to the display that would be helpful:
* tab completion & suggestion
    * this could probably reuse the UI paradigm that we use to autocomplete with multiple options in other cases
    * these suggestions could be based on a number of things:
         * all tags previously used in the notebook
         * core supported tags (e.g., those like `jupyter:hide_cell` in nbconvert) 
         * tags used by extensions (or at least a list that extensions can write to for getting suggestions) 
         * some tags may be more or less appropriate for different kinds of cells (e.g., `jupyter:hide_output` doesn't mean anything for a markdown cell)
    * for the case of prefixed tags, it may be useful to suggest completions that would apply the prefix if the unprefixed text is present (e.g., `hide_cell` → `jupyter:hide_cell`)
* "clickable" suggestions (discussed more in https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1812)

One downside to this approach to applying tags is that it doesn't have any way to apply to multiple cells at the same time, since multiple cells can be selected, it'd be good to also surface a way to do that. I think that solving that might also suggest a more general UI that would work nicely (especially for applying tags via keyboard shortcut). To be fair, this is a tough problem that none of the other tagging solutions have solved, but it's still worth thinking about.

Wow, this has been running for a while.  Question--did you upgrade your software without first stopping all Blockstack processes?  Specifically, do you know if doing so upgraded any C Python libraries?  I'm not 100% sure if this is the cause, but I've had mysterious non-reproducible segfaults like this in the past where I upgraded a library by overwriting it, instead of unlinking it and then writing a new one.  (The reason this can cause a segfault is because the binary image that's mapped into RAM will change if you don't first unlink the library.  Unlinking it ensures that copying the new library to the same name allocates a new inode, and thus won't be touched by the already-running binary).

I think it's a duplicate if you only get the warning during the initial run, if I understood correctly I don't think there's a way to avoid that besides suppressing warnings as suggested in #1623. However further warnings during normal letsencrypt operations should be fixed separately using something like what's already in acme/acme/client.py:
```python
# Prior to Python 2.7.9 the stdlib SSL module did not allow a user to configure
# many important security related options. On these platforms we use PyOpenSSL
# for SSL, which does allow these options to be configured.
# https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html#insecureplatformwarning
if sys.version_info < (2, 7, 9):  # pragma: no cover
    requests.packages.urllib3.contrib.pyopenssl.inject_into_urllib3()
```
which would be the best solution possible. PyOpenSSL is already a dependency and is installed during the bootstrap process, so the fix would be to add the above lines wherever requests is imported.

@ffxsam to prove to letsencrypt that you own the domain, it needs to be able to dial-back to its client running on that domain. If you can't make letsencrypt run where the domain is you can't prove you own it.

You could do this unreasonably difficult process instead:

# temporarily change your DNS to point at a server you do control, perhaps your house
# (perhaps) set up port forwards for 80 and 443 if you're behind a NAT (like, at your house)
# run letsencrypt with `certonly` (or `webroot`, but I think `certonly` is easier)
# change your DNS back and upload the certs

Renewal becomes irritating because you need to do this every three months, which means breaking your DNS every three months, which means at least a half hour and maybe a day of downtime for your domain, unless you also set up a proxy server after you get the certs. The whole point of letsencrypt is to automate that stuff away, so this method isn't really reasonable. I think there are cPanel plugins in the works for integrating letsencrypt on the remote server. Maybe you can bug your host to install them.

All messages accepted by produce() end up in an internal queue, if the leader broker is up for the given partition the messages are transmitted to the broker within queue.buffering.max.ms.
If no leader is available the message remains in queue until a new leader is elected and comes online or the message times out (message.timeout.ms) in which case on_delivery will give you TIMED_OUT or TIMED_OUT_QUEUE.

You don't need to all flush() to make any of this happen, it is all handled automatically in the background. flush() just *waits* until all messages are either succesfully produced or permanently fails, calling any registered callbacks while doing so.
flush() is typically used at program shutdown to make sure all outstanding messages are delivered or failed before terminating.


I see in your consumer code that you are trying to handle error conditions by "reconnecting" by re-subscribing to your topic set. You don't need to do this, librdkafka will automatically try to recover from temporary errors. There's been a bunch of fixes for these types of error cases in the latest librdkafka version v0.9.4. I suggest you upgrade librdkafka to 0.9.4 and see if you are still seeing the same problems, also remove the re-subscription code, you don't need it.

What do you mean by “custom color for 00C4FF”? Powerline uses highlight groups which refer to colorscheme “colors” which are (unless being gradients) either cterm color indexes or 2-tuples (lists with two items in JSON) with cterm color index and RGB value. There are lots of examples of both in `powerline/config_files/colors.json`. Highlight group definitions never refer to some particular color directly though, all colors need to be defined in `colors.json`.

@rockmnew I am currently using this work around. Make sure `strict = True` in `class Meta`.

    from marshmallow import Schema
    from marshmallow_jsonapi.schema import Schema as DefaultSchema

    class BaseSchema(DefaultSchema):
        def _do_load(self, data, many=None, **kwargs):
            """Override `marshmallow.Schema._do_load` for custom JSON API handling.

            Specifically, we do this to format errors as JSON API Error objects,
            and to support loading of included data.
            """
            # TODO: Pull request to support marshmallow 3.X
            many = self.many if many is None else bool(many)

            # Store this on the instance so we have access to the included data
            # when processing relationships (``included`` is outside of the
            # ``data``).
            self.included_data = data.get('included', {})

            try:
                result = super(Schema, self)._do_load(data, many, **kwargs)
            except ValidationError as err:  # strict mode
                error_messages = err.messages
                if '_schema' in error_messages:
                    error_messages = error_messages['_schema']
                formatted_messages = self.format_errors(error_messages, many=many)
                err.messages = formatted_messages
                raise err

            return result

I found the bug. When you resolve the stack reference in `get_stack_refs`, you try to open a senza file with the name given and if that succeeds, you fetch the name from the file. You never check if the file is a valid senza file. Coincidentally I had a random file with the same name as my stack in my home so when resolving the stack reference, that file would be opened but it would not contain any valid senza definitions and that explains why the lookup fails.

In order to mitigate that, you could check for the existence of the keys you try to lookup or you could add `TypeError` to the catch block. Not sure what's cleaner since I'm not Python expert. :)

In general, Z3 interleaves quantifier instantiation with a search procedure, i.e. small changes to the input may have a huge impact on the result and the time required to get there. In this particular case, it's the array theory that gives up. 

The simplifier rewrites the additional quantifiers like so (run `(apply simplify)` instead of `(check-sat)`):

```
  (forall ((a (Array Int integer)) (old_first Int) (new_first Int))
    (forall ((i Int))
      (! (= (select (slide a old_first new_first) i)
            (select a (+ i (* (- 1) new_first) old_first)))
         :pattern ((select (slide a old_first new_first) i)))))
```

And the solver then proceeds to instantiate the _outer_ quantifier, for which it automatically inferred the pattern `((slide a old_first new_first))`. It then instantiates the (still outer) quantifier, resulting in an additional quantified term over `i` and containing an array term. Similarly, the inferred patterns for the quantifiers at lines 44 and 64 contain array terms (`((select tab x))` and `((select (select tab x) y))`).

Non-incremental mode eliminates x and creates a model x = bvudiv0(1). 
Incremental mode invokes the theory_bv and treats bvudiv0 as uninterepreted (as it should), but
since bvudiv0 is a built-in symbol, no entry is generated for it during model construction, so when the
model evaluator picks up (bvudiv 1 0) and rewrites it first to (bvdiv0 1) it is unable to evaluate it further.
So it is unable to validate that the interpretation that sets (bvdiv0 1) to 0 is legal.

The issue I'm describing isn't that the pattern is invalid, but that the observed data is invalid.  I only mention running the pattern matcher to demonstrate that the values I'm talking about are invalid.  So I'll start over and try to state it more simply.

If a STIX observed data's `objects` property contains an `artifact` object with a `payload_bin` property, [it's contents should be base64 encoded](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1b7ZahfoxIepkv3MoaxGvA4WUq6GaCl5S_VPKpIEEAm4/edit#heading=h.4jegwl6ojbes) and so such an observed data artifact should fail validation if the value is not base64 encoded.  Currently such values do not fail validation.  For example...
```
{
  "type": "artifact", 
  "mime_type": "application/octet-stream",
  "payload_bin": "I am not base64 encoded"
}
```

...should fail validation but currently it succeeds.

I stated it more broadly above because [`cti-pattern-matcher` decodes *all* properties whose names end with `_bin` or `_hex`](https://github.com/oasis-open/cti-pattern-matcher/blob/master/stix2matcher/matcher.py#L280).  So on that count it seems like either `cti-pattern-matcher` should be more explicit in which properties it decodes, or the this validator should be more broad in which properties it requires to be encoded.

> Sure, we could do that, but it'd make the intended use-case a bit more complicated.

We'd have to do that as an additional option anyway, not as a replacement. It could also become a super class, but we probably don't want a `CustomCustomRecurrentLayer` and it would make it more difficult to keep the current `precompute_input` behaviour then -- this is way easier to implement if you know about the split of `in_to_hid` and `hid_to_hid`.

>> That would already allow feeding back the previous output of a recurrent layer.

> What do you mean by this?

One of the use cases was a decoder that gets to see its prediction from the previous time step. With the current `CustomRecurrentLayer` this is not possible exactly because of the split -- there is no way for a custom computation to depend on both the current input and the past hidden state. With a `in_and_hid_to_hid` network, users could add something that sees both the current input and the past hidden state (where part of the past hidden state could be the past prediction).

> How would you get the shape after each encoding Layer in this case?

The only safe way is to have your decoding layers be `MergeLayer` instances that get input both from their direct incoming layer and from the layer they need the symbolic input shape of. The `InverseLayer` could be just what you need if we implement #447.

> feels like quite a natural thing to have if you support multiple outputs, and could then be a useful thing to build on and re-use since it would own the logic for getting combined outputs/params/all layers.

But did you fully appreciate the impact of what happens if you do `[l.get_output() for l in my_output_layers]` instead of `get_output(my_output_layers)`? (#97, #104) Having `get_output()` as a `Layer` method would invite this use, reopening a pitfall that we intended to close. It would require memoization then, with all its disadvantages (memoizing multiple variants for the different keyword arguments and input layers, taking care about properly using weak references to avoid leaks, increasing code complexity).

Hi all. I have a desire to make layers with multiple outputs. (My use-case I currently implement as two different Layer classes which share parameters with each other, but it's a clunky OO workaround for something which is conceptually a single feature, and should be a single class holding its own data and providing two outputs to go down different branches of the DAG. It makes it feel like Lasagne can't "really" do arbitrary DAG, which it should :))

My two pence on @f0k's [proposals of 16th Dec](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/337#issuecomment-165122642):

* I very much like the approach of `SplitLayer` and  `Layer.__index__`. Because `cell = layer['cell']` is indeed how I want to program my DAG.
* Could we perhaps enforce that multiple-output layers should return Dicts, not Lists? That would avoid the list-vs-tuple ugliness, which I think is worth avoiding because otherwise users will have some painful experiences.

(This recent proposal is much nicer than things mentioned higher up the thread such as side-outputs.)

> Would the Lasagne community / developers be interested in adding code for training loops, basic DNN construction, etc?

Yes! This is actually among our earliest issues: #12
Thank you for pushing this!

> I found that code for training loops with features such as early termination, etc would involve a lot of copy+paste, so I put together a library [...]
> I would be happy to collaborate in order to incorporate it into Lasagne if you're up for it.

We probably all have our own experiment framework built around Lasagne to avoid copy/pasting code. If we are to integrate training loops into Lasagne, ideally, they should be both easy to use for beginners and flexible enough so our frameworks on top can use them (that's not really a requirement -- the frameworks work as they are -- but more like a gauge for generalizability). So we need to be quite careful about the interface. This being said, what you describe seems like a very good start, more general than #599.

> Code for iterating though a dataset looks like:
```
for (batch_X, batch_y) in data_source.batch_iterator([trainX, train_y], batchsize=128)
    ...
```
where `train_X` and `train_y` can be NumPy arrays or other objects that support `__len__` and `__getitem__`;

I haven't looked at the code, but it seems it supports an arbitrary number of inputs, right? (This is a strict requirement.) The interface of `__len__` and `__getitem__` seems good, I can easily wrap my own framework to be compatible to that.

> Defines the `Trainer` class

Looking at your example, it's not entirely clear why this should be a class, not a function -- couldn't this just be a `train()` function with a bunch of parameters? Do you need to access the trainer after training? I'm not yet saying it shouldn't be a class, just trying to understand what we need.
The `train_with`, `evaluate_with` interface reads nice, but shouldn't this all be part of the constructor (or function arguments, if reducing this to a function)? What happens in `train` if I omit one of these calls?
Furthermore, I'm not 100% sure about requiring the data and iteration function to be split up. Some users may have a generator function for the data that they would like to pass directly. Of course, this then requires some way to pass the epoch size (a feature I'd like to see in any case, as I often train on mini-epochs), and some way to restart the validation set iterator (or pass its size as well).
It should also be possible to perform some custom action after each epoch, like adjusting the learning rate. One way would be a callback function (in my framework, the callbacks gets passed the epoch count and the error history, and return whether training should stop -- this allows for learning rate adjustment, custom validation, and early stopping). Another, possibly simpler one would be providing a function for the inner training loop, doing one epoch, and another function wrapping it. Then users can decide whether to use the simple wrapper, or write the outer loop themselves. This is simpler to grasp than callback functions. (We could also have both.)

> Defines the `BasicDNN` class that defines a neural network that can have multiple inputs and multiple targets.

It would be great to have a way to reduce the boilerplate for setting up the training and validation functions. But I think I don't like giving everything off to a class with a `train()` method -- this creates a lot of intransparency. Have a look at https://github.com/fchollet/keras/blob/70ebb15/keras/models.py#L557 and see how much you need to read to see what's going on, and to begin understanding how you can modify it. Thinking about it, I guess the key to transparency is returning to user code as often as feasible. So I'd rather provide a function that sets up the training and validation functions and returns them. This makes it easy for users to mix and match what to use from Lasagne, and what to implement themselves, without having to write any custom classes.

> Uses objectives defined in the `dnn_objective` module to provide targets to optimise and generate the loss function.

Anything we integrate into lasagne should probably just use `lasagne.objectives`.

> `image_window_extractor`

That might be something to keep out of Lasagne. It's hard to draw a line otherwise -- what about data augmentation, tools for inputting text or audio instead of images, ...?

> `work_pool`

Again, any kind of data processing should probably be kept out of Lasagne. One of the design goals is to "do one thing and do it well". The idea would be to have the training loop code in Lasagne be general enough to work with any kind of any iteration, so your image patch iteration and multi-processing could live in its own independent module.

> `pretrained`

It would be cool to turn `Recipes` into an installable module (https://github.com/Lasagne/Recipes/issues/18), with an interface that makes it easy to access the model zoo. This could be a good basis.

---

So, to conclude: Yes, we're very interested, but we need to discuss the details of how to structure the implementation so it's generally useful. Alternatively, we can also discuss whether it *needs* to be generally useful, or just capture the most basic use cases. Let me know what you think about my comments above, I'm happy to discuss the interface in detail!

```
Django<2.0
django-import-export

Then:

I've digged deeper in this problem, and I think that the VEX output is not equivalent with the machine code above:

- the range of `si` can go from `[0x0000-0xFFFF]`
- the jump is taken in the range `[0x0080-0x7FFF]`, and not taken in `[0x8000-0xFFFF, 0x0000-0x007F]`, since `jg` is signed comparison.
- the VEX code does an unsigned extension to this at `03`: 16Uto32, so the value of `t3` will be in the range from `[0x00000000-0x0000FFFF]`
- now independent if we do signed or unsigned comparison in `x86g_calculate_condition`, the branches will be taken in `[0x00000080-0x0000FFFF]`, and not taken in `[0x00000000-0x0000007F]`

I think this is due to the signal extension (to generate the last few elements of the decomposition, the wavelet has to be multiplied with values *past* the end of the signal). For SWT, the `periodization` mode is used, so the samples at the very end are influenced by those at the beginning.

Insertion of bs and dos can be done using mmdb(object of VaspDb) without using the task. Instead of lumping the dos and bs insertion into the insert method separate methods are provided for bs and dos insertion i.e inserting dos and bs along the rest of the task doc is currently a 3 step process:
mmdb.insert_gridfs --> bs
mmdb.insert_gridfs --> dos
mmdb.insert --> the rest of the basic vasp output stuff

the insert method is inherited from the base class calcdb and the other 2 methods are defined only for vaspdb (subclasses​ calcdb). This is done so that the user can define their own inherited db class with customized vassp output data insertion , with the insert method only inserting the basic stuff by default...an example for such use case would be boltzrtrapdb that does some processing on the bandstructure before insertion(@computron and @shyamd would be able to comment more on this)...

It actually doesn't work because  OptimizeFW doesn't know to copy over the CONTCAR from the previous calculation if it has parents or `calc_locs` specified.

You might suggest to get the same behavior by overriding the input set for the StaticFW and using those. However current way StaticFW works is also kind of wonky because if there are parents with `prev_calc_dir` or `calc_locs` specified, it will actually throw away the input set you pass to StaticFW and force  you to use `MPStatic.from_prev_calc` set with all those parameters applied for you and you get some control by passing `vasp_input_set_params` to the StaticFW.

Custodian is fine in this example, but if you want any control flow or the ability to handle ISIF 4 failing to write some intermediate result to a database without redoing ISIF 2 with Fireworks, then you're out of luck.

I know this is a contrived example, but it's also a real thing that I implemented and worked around.

ramcdougal [19 minutes ago] 
short answer is that it probably doesn't matter

ramcdougal [15 minutes ago] 
mathematical answer is that: if you're using the Mersenne Twister (Python's default prng) and you set, say, seed(1) vs seed(2) what you're doing is jumping into different places in a single random stream. (There's a scrambling that happens that makes the locations not adjacent, but it's still the same stream... pull enough bits and they'll align.)

salvadord [14 minutes ago]

ramcdougal [13 minutes ago] 
This is why NEURON uses Random123 which is a cryptographic quality generator (Mersenne Twister is not) that creates independent random streams based on 3 ints. The usual way to do this is to have a global seed and then a per cell stream id.

For the comp bio course here, I recommended the `randomstate` module which also provides mathematically independent random streams

> All of the functions in this module share a common structure. The first argument is a [`pulp_smash.config.ServerConfig`](http://pulp-smash.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/pulp_smash.config.html#pulp_smash.config.ServerConfig), and all other arguments correspond to command-line options. Most arguments are named after a flag. For example, an argument `to_repo_id` corresponds to the flag `--to-repo-id`.
>
> For the meaning of each argument, see pulp-admin.

Here is another good candidate algorithm for the sequential learning part: http://blog.dlib.net/2017/12/a-global-optimization-algorithm-worth.html

Most (all?) of those sequential learning algorithms will yield one next suggestion at a time though. They have to be tweaked to be cast into: given current state give me the next n_workers to explore approximately in parallel next. It's probably doable to hack to tabu search heuristics such as:

- give state `s_t`, what is the next best guess `g_(t, 0) = make_guess(s_t)`, schedule `eval(g_(t, 0))`,
- while waiting for  `eval(g_(t, 0))` to happen, let's pretend that it has returned some average/median value `e_(t, 0)` and ask for the next "second best guess" `g_(t, 1) = make_guess(s_t \union e_(t, 0))` and schedule `eval(g_(t, 1))` to run in parallel of `eval(g_(t, 0))`
- continue pretending that you did not get the result of any evaluation as long as there are free workers by generating third, fourth, ... best guesses.

As soon as we get the true result of any evaluation, let's update state `s_t` into `s_(t+1)` and use that to generate the following guesses.

So you placed it there because Certbot's installation was there? While I understand that, here's what's going on on our end.

Until Certbot 0.18.0, certbot-auto installed Certbot inside the user's home directory. This was done by creating a Python virtual environment and installing Certbot inside of it. When certbot-auto upgrades, it deletes and recreates this virtual environment. While it's kind of nice to start with a clean environment each time a new version of Certbot is installed, we tried just upgrading the installation if it exists for other reasons and found this approach often didn't work. See #4047.

I think this problem was largely mitigated with Certbot 0.18.0. For new certbot-auto users, we never touch your home directory and instead put everything in `/opt`. However, when updating a certbot-auto user from a version that did put everything in your home directory and deleted it upon upgrading, we create a symlink to the new installation as some users have been running Certbot from the virtual environment directly.

Unfortunately, I don't think we want to interactively prompt the hundreds of thousands of certbot-auto about this when they upgrade, especially when most are running Certbot from something like cron once they've initially obtained their certs.

Due to the installation in `/opt`, I think this problem will fade away on its own over time, but if we wanted to do more, I think we could document this behavior and/or try to leave the top level directory of the virtual environment untouched and just delete the specific files and directories it creates. This seems error prone, I'm not sure it will always work, and obviously won't work if the user modifies the subdirectories here, but it's something we could consider.

`--pre-hook` and `--post-hook` are also only called when a certificate is due for renewal. Additionally, having a `$RENEWED_LINEAGE` variable for the pre and post hooks doesn't really make sense. `certbot renew` renews all certificates that are due for renewal, including multiple lineages. pre and post hooks are meant to run once before any certificates are renewed and once after all certificates are renewed respectively. Because these hooks are for the entire run and not individual lineages, there isn't a single value for the `$RENEWED_LINEAGE` variable.

For reference, Python has some built-in punycode support in the `encodings.idna`  module (in the standard library since Python 2.3!) https://docs.python.org/2/library/codecs.html#module-encodings.idna

But it looks like it operates on "labels", so I think you have to do

".".join(idna.ToASCII(idna.nameprep(x)) for x in d.split("."))

This worked correctly for test names that I tried. Notably, `idna.ToASCII(idna.nameprep(d))` does *not* do the right thing; for example, `idna.ToASCII(idna.nameprep(u"Schönheit.com"))` is `'xn--schnheit.com-6ib'` instead of the correct `'xn--schnheit-p4a.com'`.

(You have to apply the punycode encoding to each DNS label, not to the FQDN as a whole.)

As Mateusz pointed out, it’s not only about parentheses. I do want to expand each sub-node’s range to include parentheses, but I also want to include comments that are within the parent’s parentheses.

Consider the “ugly example” above. I will tokenize it for clarity and mark out the various ranges involved. I will also add a few extra pairs of parentheses.

Here’s the starting position. The original parent range is too small so limiting to it will not let me extend over the comment after `b`:

```
    ┌─────────────────────parent──────────────────────┐
 ( ( ( a ) COMMENT NL COMMENT NL * NL COMMENT NL ( b ) COMMENT NL ) )
      └─┘ .left                                   └─┘ .right
```

If I extend each range to include each matching pair of parentheses and only those, I get this:

```
┌───────────────────────greedy_extent(parent)────────────────────────┐
 ( ( ( a ) COMMENT NL COMMENT NL * NL COMMENT NL ( b ) COMMENT NL ) )
    └─────┘ .left                               └─────┘ .right
```

Still not greedy enough. So I extend the operands’ ranges all the way *up to but not including* the nearest significant tokens that do *not* form a pair of parentheses:

```
 ( ( ( a ) COMMENT NL COMMENT NL * NL COMMENT NL ( b ) COMMENT NL ) )
    └───────────.left───────────┘ └────────────.right────────────┘ 
```

After drawing these schematics, I do believe the parent range is not strictly necessary. Still, I’m inclined to keep it simply because it will remind my future self what the maximum possible scope of the transformation is.

```
    ┌────────────────conservative_extent(parent)─────────────────┐
 ( ( ( a ) COMMENT NL COMMENT NL * NL COMMENT NL ( b ) COMMENT NL ) )
    └───────────.left───────────┘ └────────────.right────────────┘ 
```

```python
#!/usr/bin/python3

import sys
import textwrap
import token

import asttokens


def conservative_extent(atokens, node):
    prev = atokens.prev_token(node.first_token)
    next = atokens.next_token(node.last_token)
    if (asttokens.util.match_token(prev, token.OP, '(') and
        asttokens.util.match_token(next, token.OP, ')')):
        return prev.endpos, next.startpos
    return node.first_token.startpos, node.last_token.endpos


def greedy_extent_limited(atokens, node, start_limit, end_limit):
    first = node.first_token
    last = node.last_token
    while True:
        prev = atokens.prev_token(first)
        next = atokens.next_token(last)
        if not (asttokens.util.match_token(prev, token.OP, '(') and
                asttokens.util.match_token(next, token.OP, ')') and
                prev.startpos >= start_limit and
                next.endpos <= end_limit):
            return (atokens.next_token(prev, True).startpos,
                    atokens.prev_token(next, True).endpos)
        first = prev
        last = next
    return (first.startpos, last.endpos)


def text_with_parens(atokens, node):
    startpos, endpos = range_with_parens(atokens, node)
    return atokens.text[startpos:endpos]


def test_transpose(text):
    print(text)
    print('↓')
    atokens = asttokens.ASTTokens(text, parse=True)
    node = atokens.tree.body[0].value
    start, end = conservative_extent(atokens, node)
    l_start, l_end = greedy_extent_limited(atokens, node.left, start, end)
    r_start, r_end = greedy_extent_limited(atokens, node.right, start, end)
    print(''.join((text[:l_start],
                   text[r_start:r_end],
                   text[l_end:r_start],
                   text[l_start:l_end],
                   text[r_end:])))
    print()


def main():
    test_transpose(textwrap.dedent('''\
        ((a * b + c) * d)'''))
    test_transpose(textwrap.dedent('''\
        (a # this is my lhs
        # *** end of lhs ***
        *
        # *** start of rhs
        b # this is my rhs
        )'''))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.exit(main() or 0)
```

OK, so the consensus is to

1. Bump version requirement to 3.6.
2. Remove our own string interpolation system.
3. Change script style to python format string, with option `sigil`.
4. Make `input` etc a special class to be able to use our old string conversion features.

I would suggest against using `sigil='${}'` as default because it can confuse people and it is not terribly difficult to add this option to existing script.

We can certainly implement\ the behavior (missing as standard, `None` as `DEVNULL`) but this is not very pythonic so I am trying to see if there can be a better even more general method (e.g. can handle non-path object like `DEVNULL` and allow users to pass arbitrary object with `write` attribute).

But I agree that `stdout=None` is the most obvious method for users.

Ok, I reread this a few times, and now think I am following. Just a few follow-ups to make sure.

> A step op of OpRead is issued by the engine in these cases:

Let me put some examples in here to check my understanding.

> A ReadResource occurs and the engine does not observe an old resource in the snapshot with the given URN. (Conceptually, a read-create). The engine inserts the new read resource state into the snapshot.

This happens the first time I run a Pulumi program containing a line like

```typescript
let vm = aws.ec2.Instance.read("my-vm", "i-b3476fe98");
```

> A ReadResource occurs and the engine does observe an old resource in the snapshot with the given URN, but that resource is marked External. (Conceptually, a read-update). The engine removes the existing resource state from the snapshot and inserts the new read state.

This happens all subsequent times I run that same Pulumi program.

> A step op of OpReadReplace is issued by the engine in these cases:

Here is where things get a little tricky.

> A ReadResource occurs and the engine does observe an old resource in the snapshot with the given URN, but that engine is _not_ marked External. The engine pends deletion of the existing resource and inserts the new read state into the snapshot.

This would happen if ran a Pulumi program containing this

```typescript
let vm = new aws.ec2.Instance("my-vm", { ... });
```

followed by editing that same Pulumi program and rerunning it as follows

```typescript
let vm = aws.ec2.Instance.read("my-vm", "i-b3476fe98");
```

The one case that I am unsure about is what if I do the reverse. In other words, start with

```typescript
let vm = aws.ec2.Instance.read("my-vm", "i-b3476fe98");
```

and then replace it with

```typescript
let vm = new aws.ec2.Instance("my-vm", { ... });
```

Does that simply perform an ordinary replacement operation and, because the old state is marked External, everything works out just right?

Thanks -- just want to make sure I get the flow here before diving in!

LIMIX variance decomposition supports missing data. Passing a phenotype matrix with with NaN values indicating the missing values, LIMIX will perform inference without considering the missing data. Optimisation will be much slower than the fully-observed case as the Kronecker structure of the problem cannot be exploited when missing data are present. Generally, computational complexity scales cubically with the number of observations and memory quadratically.

Okay, new thought: no package name stuff. The widget manager is responsible for loading correct versions of the models/views, full stop. Right now I'm thinking of three widget managers:

1. The notebook - widget libraries are installed and those are the ones available. It's up to the user to make sure the correct version of the library is installed.
2. JupyterLab - again, specific widget extensions are registered. It's up to the user to make sure the correct version of the library is installed.
3. The embedded widget manager - this is the only one that really uses the module_version as an npm package version. For many widgets, this will be correct. Any where it's not, the widget manager is responsible for translating module versions to package versions.

For each of these cases, the version of jupyter-js-widgets is special-cased to be the one we are extending.

So I'll strip this PR down to some of the cleanup I've done, and then the major change will be:

1. Make each widget manager completely in charge of loading the correct class for views and models. The default implementation will *not* assume require. I'm still undecided if the manager-base will provide code to load widgets from the jupyter-js-widgets package (leaning against it so that in the future, it's easier to separate out the base manager from our core widget implementation).
2. Change the documentation of the `_module_*` attributes to not refer to npm packages, but rather versions of the models/views (which can coincide with npm package names/versions by convention/coincidence).
