`ensure`: ensure that it exists in the index (adding it if needed)

`verify` (default): ensure that it exists in the index (adding it if needed), and throw an error if any metadata differs from what we already have.

`skip`: Confusingly named: it skips adding the sources, but will still link them to this dataset.
- From a user perspective, `skip` really means "throw an error if we don't already have all the sources".
- Which is slightly more useful than it sounds: most of our processing is on datasets that are already in the cube, so if it's quietly adding new sources we'd want to know about it.
    - Example previous bug: an old scene processor that inappropriately scanned the filesystem for datasets to process was picking up datasets that were partially-written/corrupt.

I can explain what is happening, but I'm not really sure exactly what we should be doing different.

First `jep.eval()` is using `Py_single_input`, which means if the expression evaluates to an object then it will be printed. I'm not a big fan of this behavior but I think the interactive jep interpreter is relying on it.

With `setRedirectOutputStreams(false)` the python output will just go to stdout. If you don't change your Java `System.out` then usually Python and Java are printing to the same place. When I try to reproduce your issue on the command line then I always see all the `array(...)` output for each iteration of the loop. When I redirect the output to a file then I see your issue, although the cutoff for me is 112 times through the loop. The issue is that internally python is buffering the output, when it detects a terminal then it is line buffered so all the output gets flushed as long as it ends in newline, but when I redirect to a file then it is using an 8k buffer so it only flushes if I write more than 8k. It appears when you close jep that we just discard any buffered output. I am guessing that you are running in an IDE or some other environment that is doing some redirection of stdout that causes python to switch to the 8k buffer mode. I was not able to repeat this on python 2, it always prints everything. I looked at the python source and I didn't pinpoint the exact change but it is obvious they shuffled around much of the related logic.

As I mentioned I'm not sure what we should do, a few different things that occurred to me;
1) On `Jep.close()` we could flush sys.stdout to ensure that the buffer is not lost. This would result in always seeing the output. We should also update the javadoc on `Jep.eval()` to indicate that it prints the result.
2) If Jep is not interactive then perhaps we should not be printing the result of `jep.eval()`, which we could do if we stop using `Py_single_input`. I think this is more inline with what you want and I think it makes sense, however I am worried that one of the other modes would have other subtle differences that would make it harder to debug through the interactive interpreter so need to do more research.
3) We could expose the eval mode, either as a method arg or by providing alternative methods that use different modes. This could be similar to the python builtin [compile](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#compile) perhaps? We could probably knock out #55 if we did this well.

Is the image data in a numpy.ndarray?  If so, you could just use `NDArray image = (NDArray) jep.getValue(String nameOfPythonVariable)` and you'd get back a Java NDArray and be able to get the bytes out of that with `image.getData()`.  If it's not a numpy.ndarray you could turn it into one and get the bytes back the same way.

Another potential way is to use `jep.getValue_bytearray(String nameOfPythonVariable)' to get back the bytes.  That method may go away in a future version of the API but if it goes away there will probably be a way to do the same thing, just different API.

Which command do you use to start LocalStack, and how do you create and invoke your Lambda - I'm assuming you're running in docker-compose with `LAMBDA_EXECUTOR=docker`, and that your code sets the environment variable `endpoint` to `fake-aws`, correct?

The problem is that if Lambdas are executed in Docker (`LAMBDA_EXECUTOR=docker`), each function is running in a separate container which is not part of the docker-compose stack, and hence cannot access its network.

We have the special purpose environment variable `LOCALSTACK_HOSTNAME`, see [README](https://github.com/localstack/localstack#configurations). Can you try using that as the endpoint for Elasticsearch?

The difference between ES and Elasticsearch is: ES is the API that allows to create Elasticsearch domains, manage cluster configuration etc., and Elasticsearch (port 4571) is the actual Elasticsearch database endpoint. In other words, LocalStack currently only supports a single domain and it always runs on the same port (4571).

My concern here is that if someone has a 32 core machine, `DEFAULT_NPARTITIONS = 64`, and there would be 64*64 = 4096 blocks in a large enough DataFrame. Any operation on blocks, such as `abs` would deploy 4096 remote tasks; this would be very costly.

We should probably do more testing on different machines, before deciding on a good heuristic for npartitions.

Check the logs, that's what they are there for (`~/.ipython/profile_default/log`).  You can see the individual PIDs of the controller and engine if you start `ipcluster --debug`.

When debugging, *always* go back to starting engines and controller manually (with `ipcontroller --debug` and `ipengine --debug`) and see if you can reproduce the error without ipcluster.

Also note that the Controller is actually a collection of five processes, so ten processes does not suggest that there are eight engines.  Assuming client is running, then at most three engines are alive (10 - ipcontroller (5) - ipcluster - client = 3).
